initialPosition.legs: Lockdown: your outside leg hooks over opponent's trapped ankle, your inside leg hooks under and locks it (figure-four on their lower leg). Extending to stretch their leg.
initialPosition.hips: Extending away from opponent to stretch their leg and break their base.

greenLights: Lockdown is established — double leg entanglement on their trapped leg
You can extend and stretch their leg, breaking their posture
Overhook/whizzer is controlling their upper body
Opponent's base is disrupted by the lockdown pressure
redLights: Lockdown hasn't been established — need both legs configured correctly
Opponent has freed their ankle from the lockdown
You can't extend — opponent is too heavy or your legs are too fatigued
Opponent has moved to a position where the lockdown has no leverage
steps: From half guard, establish the lockdown: outside leg hooks over their trapped ankle, inside leg hooks under and locks. Your legs form a figure-four on their lower leg.
EXTEND your legs to stretch their trapped leg away from their body. This breaks their base and posture.
Secure an overhook on the near side. Use the overhook and lockdown extension to create the sweeping angle.
Elevator sweep: bridge and roll toward the overhook side while extending the lockdown. Their disrupted base allows the sweep to top position.
complexpieces: Chain with electric chair: if the sweep is blocked, transition to the electric chair sweep/submission
Use the lockdown stretch to set up sweeps — they try to relieve the stretch, creating sweep openings
Transition to dog fight position by releasing the lockdown and coming up to knees
simplifications: Practice establishing the lockdown as an isolated leg configuration drill
Drill the extension mechanic — stretch their leg and feel their base break
Focus on the overhook control before adding the sweep — the upper body control makes sweeps work

techniques.name: Back Take from Half Guard
techniques.goal: Use the underhook and angle from half guard to come behind the opponent and take their back.

initialPosition.body: In half guard with a strong underhook, coming up to a seated or kneeling position. Angling behind the opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Deep underhook on the near side — arm deep under their armpit. Other hand assists by gripping belt, hip, or posting.
initialPosition.legs: Half guard entanglement on the near leg. Releasing the entanglement as you move behind them.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to the side and behind the opponent, circling to their back.

greenLights: You have a deep underhook and can come up to a kneeling/seated position
Opponent's back is accessible — they haven't turned to face you
Your underhook angle allows you to circle behind rather than sweep forward
Opponent is defending the sweep and leaving their back exposed
redLights: Opponent has a strong whizzer preventing you from coming up
They've turned to face you — back is no longer accessible
Crossface pressure is keeping you flat and unable to angle behind
Your underhook is too shallow to control the angle
steps: From half guard, establish a DEEP underhook. Come up to seated or kneeling position, driving your shoulder into their armpit.
Circle BEHIND the opponent rather than sweeping forward. Use the underhook to guide yourself around to their back.
Release the half guard entanglement as you move behind. Establish seatbelt grip (one arm over shoulder, one under armpit).
Insert hooks and complete the back take. Secure full back control with seatbelt and both hooks in.
complexpieces: Chain: underhook sweep attempt (they block) → redirect to back take since their defense creates the angle
If the back take is blocked, transition to a single leg takedown
From the back, immediately attack chokes (RNC, bow and arrow)
simplifications: Practice coming up from half guard and circling behind as a movement drill
Drill the transition from underhook to seatbelt grip without hooks first
Focus on the circling motion — go AROUND them, not over them

techniques.name: John Wayne Sweep
techniques.goal: Off-balance the opponent forward while controlling a sleeve and leg, then sit up and rotate your body using your hook to roll them over sideways.

initialPosition.body: On your back in half guard. Controlling opponent's sleeve and leg, preparing to sit up and rotate.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve grip on opponent's far arm pulling it across. Other hand controls their near leg or pant cuff.
initialPosition.legs: Half guard entanglement on the near leg. Inside hook assists the rolling motion as you sweep.
initialPosition.hips: Bridging and rotating to roll the opponent over the hooking leg side.

greenLights: You have sleeve control on the far arm — preventing them from posting
Opponent's weight is forward and their base is compromised
Your inside hook can engage for the rolling sweep
Opponent is focused on passing and not defending the sweep
redLights: Opponent's far arm is free and they can post to block
They have a strong base and aren't leaning forward
Your half guard entanglement is loose — no anchor for the hook
Crossface pressure is keeping you flat and unable to sit up
steps: From half guard, grip opponent's far sleeve and pull it across their body. Control their near leg or pant cuff with your other hand.
Bridge and sit up while PULLING their sleeve across. This off-balances them forward and prevents posting.
Rotate your body using the inside hook — roll the opponent sideways over the hook. The sleeve control kills their base.
Complete the sweep as they roll over. Come up to top position and establish control.
complexpieces: If they resist by pulling the arm back, redirect to an underhook sweep
Chain with kimura: the sleeve control can transition to a kimura figure-four on the same arm
Use the John Wayne sweep threat to set up back takes — they defend the sweep and expose the back
simplifications: Practice the sleeve grip and pull across motion as an isolated drill
Drill the sit-up and rotation from half guard with a cooperative partner
Focus on preventing the far arm from posting — that's the key to making this sweep work

techniques.name: Dog Fight Sweep
techniques.goal: Secure underhooks and come up to the dog fight position (both wrestlers on knees), then use leverage and body positioning to sweep the opponent.

initialPosition.body: Coming up from half guard to the dog fight position — both you and opponent on your knees, chest-to-chest. Underhook battle position.
initialPosition.grips: Deep underhook on the near side. Other arm fights for a second underhook, controls their hip, or grips the far leg.
initialPosition.legs: Half guard entanglement partially released — transitioning to knees. May maintain a butterfly-style hook.
initialPosition.hips: Driving into opponent from the kneeling position, competing for inside positioning.

greenLights: You've come up from half guard to dog fight — both on knees
You have a strong underhook — your arm is deeper than their whizzer
Opponent's posture is disrupted and they're not stabilized
You can drive forward with your hips and shoulder
redLights: Opponent has a dominant whizzer killing your underhook
You can't get up to the kneeling position — crossface keeping you down
Opponent has already sprawled and is driving you back to flat
Both underhooks lost and they have inside position
steps: From half guard, win the underhook and come up to your knees — entering the dog fight position.
Drive your shoulder INTO opponent's chest/armpit. Control their posture with the underhook. Fight for dominant inside position.
Bridge and drive to one side while pulling their upper body with the underhook. Use your lower body leverage to roll them.
Complete the sweep to top position. Establish side control, mount, or transition to a back take.
complexpieces: If the sweep is blocked, immediately go for the back take around the whizzer side
Chain with knee tap: from dog fight, reach for far knee to sweep sideways
If opponent re-guards during the dog fight, address the new guard before continuing
simplifications: Practice the transition from flat half guard to dog fight as a positional drill
Drill the underhook drive from kneeling position
Focus on getting your head INSIDE (on the underhook side) — head outside means they guillotine

techniques.name: Kimura (from Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's far arm with a kimura figure-four grip when they post or reach while in your half guard.

initialPosition.body: On your back or side in half guard. Opponent has posted a hand or reached forward — far arm is exposed.
initialPosition.grips: Kimura figure-four: one hand grips opponent's wrist, other reaches OVER their arm and grabs your own wrist.
initialPosition.legs: Half guard entanglement maintained. Legs control their base while you attack the arm.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the kimura side, creating torque on the trapped arm.

greenLights: Opponent posts a hand on the mat to resist a sweep — arm is isolated
You can grab their wrist and secure the figure-four grip
Half guard entanglement prevents them from pulling away
Opponent is focused on passing and not defending their arm
redLights: Opponent's arms are tight to their body — nothing to isolate
They immediately grab their belt or pants (kimura defense)
Your half guard is being passed while you attack — position before submission
You can't reach their wrist from your current angle
steps: From half guard, identify the far arm when opponent posts or reaches. Grab their wrist with your near hand.
Reach your other hand OVER their arm and grab your own wrist — kimura figure-four secured.
Pull their elbow tight to your chest. Control the arm close to your body.
Rotate their wrist behind their back with smooth pressure on the shoulder joint. If needed, hip escape to create a better finishing angle.
complexpieces: If they resist the kimura, use the grip to sweep them — the kimura grip is a powerful sweeping handle
Chain: sweep attempt → they post → kimura on posted arm → they defend → hip bump sweep
Transition to armbar or triangle if they straighten the arm to escape
simplifications: Practice recognizing the posted arm opportunity during half guard exchanges
Drill the figure-four grip quickness from half guard bottom
Focus on keeping their elbow tight to your body — space kills the kimura

techniques.name: Guillotine Choke (from Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Catch the opponent's neck with a guillotine choke when they drive forward into your half guard with their head low.

initialPosition.body: On your back in half guard. Opponent is driving forward with their head low, pressuring into you.
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm wraps around opponent's neck — wrist bone under their throat (chin strap). Other hand clasps to lock the grip.
initialPosition.legs: Half guard entanglement keeps them close and prevents escape. May transition to closed guard or full guard for extra control.
initialPosition.hips: Angling slightly to the choking side. Elevating hips for choke pressure.

greenLights: Opponent drives forward with their head low — neck exposed
You can wrap the neck with wrist bone placement under the throat
Half guard entanglement prevents them from pulling away
Opponent is focused on passing and not defending the neck
redLights: Opponent keeps head up with strong posture — neck not accessible
You can't wrap deep enough for wrist bone placement
They're pulling away and your half guard can't keep them close
Your grip is on the chin/jaw rather than the throat — won't finish
steps: From half guard, as opponent drives forward with low head, wrap your arm around their neck. Wrist bone under their throat.
Clasp hands to lock the guillotine grip (palm-to-palm or Marcelotine).
Half guard legs keep them close. Angle slightly to the choking side.
SQUEEZE by pulling elbows toward your chest while lifting hips UP. The compression chokes the arteries.
complexpieces: If they defend by turning their head, switch to arm-in guillotine for more control
Chain: guillotine attempt (they posture) → immediately sweep as their arms defend the choke
Transition to d'arce choke if the guillotine angle shifts and their arm comes inside
simplifications: Practice the chin strap grip — wrist bone placement is everything
Drill catching the neck as they drive forward from half guard
Focus on the squeeze mechanic: elbows IN, hips UP — not a neck crank

techniques.name: Arm-in Guillotine (from Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Trap the opponent's arm inside the guillotine from half guard for additional choking pressure and control.

initialPosition.body: On your back in half guard with opponent's head low. Their near arm is trapped INSIDE the guillotine alongside their neck.
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm wraps around their neck AND near arm. Wrist bone on throat. Other hand clasps to lock.
initialPosition.legs: Half guard entanglement keeps them anchored. May transition to closed guard or high guard for extra control.
initialPosition.hips: Angling to the choking side. Hips elevate for choke pressure.

greenLights: Opponent dives forward with head low and near arm is inside the wrap
Wrist bone is still on the throat with the arm trapped inside
Half guard legs keep them from pulling away
The trapped arm blocks their primary defense
redLights: You can't trap their arm inside — it's too retracted
The arm inside pushes your wrist off the throat — grip misaligned
Opponent is pulling away and half guard can't keep them close
Grip fatigue is setting in and you can't maintain pressure
steps: From half guard, as opponent drives forward, wrap your arm around their neck AND their near arm. Trap the arm inside.
Position wrist bone under their throat. Clasp hands to lock (Marcelotine or palm-to-palm).
Fall to the choking-arm side. Half guard legs keep them anchored, or transition to closed guard for more control.
SQUEEZE elbows toward your chest and ELEVATE hips. Trapped arm's shoulder adds pressure to the neck.
complexpieces: If the arm-in doesn't finish, release the arm for standard guillotine
Chain with sweep: use head control to sweep to mount and finish from top
Switch to anaconda or d'arce if the arm positioning shifts during the attempt
simplifications: Practice trapping the arm inside the wrap as a grip drill from half guard
Drill the difference between arm-in and standard guillotine positioning
Focus on wrist bone placement — the arm must not push your wrist away from the throat

techniques.name: D'Arce Choke (from Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Thread your arm under the opponent's neck and through their near arm from half guard top, applying a figure-four choking grip and squeezing with chest pressure.

initialPosition.body: In top half guard position. Opponent is on their side with underhook — their near arm and neck create the d'arce channel.
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm threads UNDER opponent's neck and through their near armpit — coming out the other side. Lock figure-four: grab your own bicep.
initialPosition.legs: In top half guard — leg still trapped but the choke works from here. May free leg during the choke.
initialPosition.hips: Driving chest pressure down and into the choke. Hips may sprawl for additional compression.

greenLights: Opponent has an underhook from bottom — their arm creates the channel for the d'arce
You can thread your arm under their neck and through their armpit
Opponent's head and arm are close together — compact channel for the choke
You can lock the figure-four grip (hand to bicep)
redLights: Opponent's head and arm are spread apart — no compact channel for the choke
You can't thread deep enough to reach the figure-four lock
Opponent is flat on their back — need them on their side for the d'arce to work
Your choking arm isn't deep enough and you're cranking the neck instead
steps: From top half guard, identify the d'arce channel — opponent's underhook creates the opening between their head and arm.
Thread your near arm UNDER their neck and through their near armpit. Drive your arm deep until you can reach the other side.
Lock the figure-four: grab your own bicep with the threading hand. Other hand goes behind their head or neck.
SQUEEZE elbows together and apply chest pressure. Walk your hips toward their head for maximum compression. The choke compresses both carotids.
complexpieces: Set up from crossface: drive the crossface deep until it threads through for the d'arce
Chain with anaconda: if the d'arce angle is wrong, switch to anaconda by adjusting the arm path
If the choke doesn't finish, use the head/arm control to pass half guard to side control
simplifications: Practice threading the arm from top half guard as an isolated movement
Drill the figure-four lock (hand to bicep) from the threaded position
Focus on the chest pressure — the squeeze comes from your chest driving into them, not just arm strength

techniques.name: Ezekiel Choke (from Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Slip one arm across the opponent's neck while securing the sleeve or wrist with the other hand from top half guard, pressing the forearm into the throat with chest pressure.

initialPosition.body: In top half guard. Chest pressure on the opponent. One arm threading across their neck for the choke.
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm: forearm or fist across opponent's throat. Other hand: feeds through your own sleeve (Gi) or locks the wrist (No-Gi).
initialPosition.legs: In top half guard — leg still trapped. The choke works from this position with sufficient chest pressure.
initialPosition.hips: Driving downward pressure through your chest into the opponent. Low and heavy.

greenLights: You have dominant top pressure in half guard — opponent is flat
You can slip your arm across their neck without losing position
Your other hand can feed through your sleeve (Gi) or control the wrist (No-Gi)
Opponent is defending the pass and not expecting the choke
redLights: Opponent has strong frames preventing you from getting close enough
Your arm can't reach across their neck — they're framing your arm away
You're losing top position while attempting the choke
Opponent tucks their chin tight and you can't get the forearm under
steps: From top half guard, establish heavy chest pressure. Flatten the opponent.
Slip your near arm across their throat — forearm or fist positioned on one side of the neck.
Feed your other hand through your own sleeve (Gi) or grab your own wrist (No-Gi) — securing the choke grip behind their head.
SQUEEZE by pressing your forearm into their throat while your other arm closes the loop behind their head. Drive chest pressure downward for maximum compression.
complexpieces: Set up during pass attempts: threaten the Ezekiel as you work to free your leg
Chain with crossface pass: if Ezekiel is defended, use the arm pressure to flatten them and pass
If they turn away to defend the choke, take the back from half guard top
simplifications: Practice the arm-across-throat and sleeve feed as an isolated grip drill
Drill maintaining heavy top pressure while threading the choke — don't rise up
Focus on the chest pressure component — the Ezekiel finishes with pressure, not just arm squeezing

techniques.name: Crossface Pressure Pass (vs Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Apply heavy crossface pressure to flatten the half guard player, prevent their underhook, and free the trapped leg to complete the pass.

initialPosition.body: In opponent's half guard on top. Driving crossface pressure with your shoulder across their jaw/face.
initialPosition.grips: Crossface arm: forearm or shoulder driving across their jaw, turning their head away. Other arm: underhook or controls their far hip.
initialPosition.legs: One leg trapped in their half guard. Free leg posts for base and drive.
initialPosition.hips: Driving LOW and FORWARD with maximum chest and shoulder pressure to flatten them.

greenLights: You can establish crossface — shoulder on their jaw turning their head away
Opponent is being flattened by the pressure
Their underhook is denied by your crossface shoulder position
The trapped leg can be freed with hip movement while maintaining pressure
redLights: Opponent has a strong underhook preventing your crossface
They're getting to their side and your crossface isn't flattening them
You're too upright — not enough pressure to control them
Opponent is transitioning to deep half as you pressure
steps: From top half guard, drive your shoulder/forearm ACROSS their jaw (crossface). Turn their head away from you.
Apply heavy chest and shoulder pressure — flatten them onto their back. Deny the underhook.
Work to free the trapped leg: hip switch, kick the leg free, or walk the knee through while maintaining crossface.
Clear the half guard entanglement and consolidate side control with crossface and hip control.
complexpieces: Chain with knee slice: use crossface to set up the slicing knee pass
If they underhook, switch to d'arce choke — their underhook creates the channel
If they get to deep half, redirect to smash pressure and address the deep position
simplifications: Practice the crossface shoulder drive as a pressure drill from top half guard
Drill freeing the trapped leg while maintaining shoulder pressure — two skills combined
Focus on staying LOW — the most common mistake is rising up and losing pressure

techniques.name: Underhook Pass (vs Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Win the underhook battle from top half guard and use it to drive forward, flattening the opponent and clearing the half guard.

initialPosition.body: In top half guard. Fighting for the underhook on the inside. Driving forward with the underhook established.
initialPosition.grips: Underhook on the near side — arm deep under opponent's armpit. Other hand controls their head, collar, or far hip.
initialPosition.legs: One leg trapped in half guard. Free leg posts for base and forward drive.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward with the underhook, using hip pressure to flatten them.

greenLights: You've won the underhook — your arm is deeper than theirs
Opponent is being flattened by the underhook drive
The underhook prevents them from getting to their side
Your weight is driving through the underhook into their body
redLights: Opponent has the dominant underhook and you're losing the battle
Your underhook is too shallow and they're overhooking it
They've transitioned to deep half using your forward pressure against you
Opponent is getting to their side despite the underhook
steps: From top half guard, fight for the underhook. Get your arm DEEP under their armpit — deeper than their underhook attempt.
Drive forward with the underhook, flattening opponent onto their back. Your shoulder drives into their chest.
Control their head with your other hand (crossface, collar grip). Prevent them from turning to their side.
Free the trapped leg using hip switching, knee extraction, or windshield wipers while maintaining underhook control. Pass to side control.
complexpieces: Chain with crossface: establish underhook, then switch to crossface for extra flattening pressure
If they get deep half while you're underhooking, transition to smash-style counter
Use the underhook to set up Ezekiel choke — your arm is already near their neck
simplifications: Practice the underhook fight from top half guard — pummeling drill
Drill driving forward with the underhook to flatten the partner
Focus on depth of the underhook — a shallow underhook gets whizzered

techniques.name: Backstep Pass (vs Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Step the trapped leg backward and around to extract it from the half guard entanglement, using the backstep to free the leg and pass.

initialPosition.body: In top half guard. Preparing to step the trapped leg backward and out of the entanglement.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's upper body — crossface, collar grip, or head control. Preventing them from following the backstep.
initialPosition.legs: Trapped leg steps BACKWARD to extract from the half guard. Free leg maintains base.
initialPosition.hips: Turning hips as you backstep, changing the angle to free the trapped leg.

greenLights: Half guard entanglement is relatively loose — the leg can be extracted
You have upper body control to prevent them from following
Your balance allows a controlled backstep without falling
Opponent is focused on their underhook, not the leg entanglement
redLights: Half guard is locked tight (lockdown) — can't extract the leg by backstepping
Your balance is compromised and the backstep would topple you
Opponent follows the backstep and maintains or improves their guard
You have no upper body control and they'll reguard immediately
steps: From top half guard, establish upper body control (crossface or collar grip). Pin them to prevent following.
Step the trapped leg BACKWARD and around. Turn your hips away from the entanglement to clear the leg.
As the leg clears, immediately close the distance — don't leave space for reguarding.
Pass to side control or transition to mount. Maintain crossface throughout the backstep.
complexpieces: Chain with knee slice: backstep to partially clear, then knee slice the rest of the way
Use the backstep to enter leg entanglements — step back into a leg lock position
If they follow with hooks, immediately hip switch and pass the other direction
simplifications: Practice the backstep extraction as a solo footwork drill
Drill backstepping from a loose half guard entanglement to learn the clearing angle
Focus on maintaining crossface during the backstep — losing it means they'll follow you

techniques.name: Hip Switch Pass (vs Half Guard)
techniques.goal: Switch your hips from one side to the other to free the trapped leg and clear the half guard using the directional change.

initialPosition.body: In top half guard. Preparing to switch hips to extract the trapped leg from the entanglement.
initialPosition.grips: Crossface or collar grip for upper body control. Other hand controls their hip or posts on the mat.
initialPosition.legs: Trapped leg will switch direction as you hip switch. Free leg adjusts base during the switch.
initialPosition.hips: Switching from one hip to the other — the directional change frees the trapped leg from the entanglement.

greenLights: You have strong crossface or upper body control
The hip switch motion can break the entanglement angle
Opponent is defending one passing direction — the switch goes the other way
Your base is stable enough for a controlled hip switch
redLights: Opponent has a tight lockdown — hip switch alone won't free the leg
You have no upper body control and the switch exposes you
Opponent follows the hip switch and reguards immediately
Your balance is compromised during the switch
steps: From top half guard with crossface control, prepare to switch your hip direction.
HIP SWITCH: rotate your hips from one side to the other in a quick, decisive motion. The trapped leg changes angle.
The directional change breaks the half guard's entanglement grip. Extract the freed leg immediately.
Complete the pass to side control on the new hip-switch side. Maintain crossface throughout.
complexpieces: Chain multiple hip switches: if one doesn't free the leg, switch back and try again — the unpredictability creates openings
Use the hip switch to set up knee slice on the new angle
If the switch creates a d'arce opportunity (their arm comes up), attack the choke immediately
simplifications: Practice the hip switch motion from top half guard as an isolated drill
Drill the hip switch with a partner offering light half guard resistance
Focus on the speed of the switch — a slow hip switch gets caught

positions.name: Mount
positions.description: Dominant top position sitting on opponent's torso. One of the highest-value positions in BJJ — multiple submissions available.

techniques.name: Americana (Keylock)
techniques.goal: Submit opponent with a shoulder lock by pinning their wrist to the mat and rotating their arm in a paint-brush arc motion against the shoulder joint.

initialPosition.body: In full mount. Weight distributed forward, chest heavy and low over opponent. Not sitting upright.
initialPosition.grips: Same-side hand pins their wrist to the mat beside their head. Other hand threads under their arm (between forearm and mat) and grabs your own wrist — figure-four grip.
initialPosition.legs: Grapevine hooks in (toes hooked under their thighs) OR knees squeezed tight to their ribs for stability.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy and low — prevents opponent from bridging you off.

greenLights: Opponent pushes on your chest with one or both hands — arm is now extended and accessible
You've pinned their wrist to the mat near their head successfully
Opponent is flat on their back, not actively bridging or shrimping
Opponent's elbow is bent at approximately 90° (americana requires a bent arm)
redLights: Opponent keeps elbows extremely tight to their body — can't isolate the arm
Opponent is actively bridging and shrimping (focus on maintaining mount first!)
Their arm is straight — americana needs a bent arm, switch to armbar instead
You're sitting too upright (easy to be bumped/bridged off)
steps: From mount, pin opponent's wrist to the mat beside their head using your same-side hand. Keep your chest heavy and head low for base.
Slide your other hand UNDER their arm (between forearm and the mat) and grab your own wrist, creating the figure-four/paintbrush grip.
Keep their elbow pinned to the mat using your body weight and chest pressure. Slowly slide their wrist toward their hip in an ARC motion along the mat.
Maintain downward pressure on the elbow while moving the wrist in the arc. The shoulder joint reaches its rotation limit — opponent taps.
complexpieces: If they straighten their arm to defend, immediately transition to a straight armbar
If they roll to their side, switch to a kimura on the exposed arm
Set up with a cross collar choke attempt: when they push your choking hand away, catch the extended arm for the americana
simplifications: Practice the figure-four grip configuration with a partner's arm repeatedly
Drill the wrist-to-hip arc to understand the correct angle of pressure
Focus first on maintaining stable mount pressure before attempting any submission

techniques.name: Ezekiel Choke
techniques.goal: Choke opponent from mount using your own gi sleeve as leverage. One arm behind their head for structure, the other feeds inside your own sleeve to press across their throat.

initialPosition.body: In mount, low and heavy on opponent's chest. Posture is forward, not sitting up.
initialPosition.grips: One arm slides behind opponent's head (under the back of their neck). The free hand reaches INSIDE the sleeve opening of the arm behind the head, knuckles presenting across the front of the throat.
initialPosition.legs: Mount with grapevines or knees tight to their ribs for stability.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy and driving forward. Weight keeps opponent flat.

greenLights: You have stable mount with heavy pressure — opponent isn't escaping
Opponent is defending by covering their neck with hands (you can wedge your arm behind their head UNDER their defensive hands)
Opponent is focused on hip escaping, not watching for the choke setup
Your arm is already behind their head — half the work is done
redLights: Opponent is actively bridging and you can't maintain mount (stabilize first)
You can't get your arm behind their head — they're blocking effectively
Your sleeve grip is too shallow to form the choke structure
Opponent has strong frames pushing you away from their upper body
steps: From mount, slide one arm behind opponent's head — under the back of their neck. Settle your weight forward and stay heavy.
With your free hand, reach inside your OWN sleeve opening of the arm behind the head. Your knuckles enter the sleeve from the inside of your forearm.
Present the knife-edge/knuckle side of your free hand across opponent's throat (forearm across the front of the neck).
Squeeze by bringing both elbows TOGETHER and dropping your weight forward. The combination of sleeve pulling and forearm pressure across the throat creates the choke.
complexpieces: Can also be applied from inside closed guard as a surprise (risky but effective)
Set up from a failed americana attempt — your arm is already behind their head from the pin
Apply from half guard top as a surprise attack when opponent is focused on sweeping
simplifications: Practice finding your own sleeve opening quickly — do it blindfolded to build muscle memory
Drill the hand-inside-sleeve mechanic against a cooperative partner or dummy
Focus on the squeeze direction: elbows together and weight forward, not pulling backward

techniques.name: Armbar from Mount
techniques.goal: Hyperextend opponent's elbow from the mounted position by transitioning to S-mount, swinging your leg over their face, and lifting your hips against their arm.

initialPosition.body: High mount with hips near opponent's chest. Transitioning to S-mount (one leg posted up near their head).
initialPosition.grips: Both hands controlling the target arm at the wrist. Other hand on their face, chest, or mat for base during the transition.
initialPosition.legs: S-mount: one knee up near opponent's head (same side as trapped arm), other leg down near their hip for base.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to face toward the trapped arm, shifting weight toward their head for the transition.

greenLights: Opponent pushes against your chest with arms extended — arm is exposed
You've isolated one arm at the wrist successfully
Opponent is flat on their back and not actively bridging
You've achieved high mount or S-mount control
redLights: Opponent keeps both arms very tight and refuses to extend — don't force it
Opponent is actively bucking and bridging (secure mount first)
You don't have wrist control — they'll pull the arm out when you spin
Opponent is turned to their side — they can escape more easily during transition
steps: From high mount, control the target arm at the wrist. Place your other hand on the mat (or their face) for base.
Step your foot up near their head on the SAME SIDE as the trapped arm, entering S-mount. Lean your weight toward their head.
Swing your other leg OVER their face. Sit back while clamping both knees together tightly. Keep their thumb pointing at the ceiling (elbow joint facing you).
Pinch knees tight, control the wrist, lift hips UP and slightly back against their elbow joint for the submission.
complexpieces: If they clasp hands to defend, use a bicep curl, wrist peel, or push their hands toward their feet
Chain: cross collar choke attempt → they push your hand → catch the pushing arm for armbar
If they try to pull the arm out during the spin, transition to triangle
simplifications: Practice the S-mount position hold before adding the armbar (just maintain the position for 30 seconds)
Drill the leg swing-over against no resistance to build the movement pattern
Break into phases: (1) S-mount entry, (2) leg over face, (3) finishing squeeze

techniques.name: Trap & Roll Escape (Upa)
techniques.goal: Bridge opponent over your body to reverse mount position — you end up in their closed guard on top. A power-based escape using explosive hip movement.

initialPosition.body: On your back underneath mount. Arms are defending and about to trap.
initialPosition.grips: Trap opponent's arm on one side: hug their wrist/forearm tight to your chest with an overhook. Other hand can frame on their hip or control their head.
initialPosition.legs: Trap opponent's foot on the SAME side as the trapped arm: step your foot over their ankle, pinning it to the mat.
initialPosition.hips: Loaded and coiled, ready to bridge explosively upward and over.

greenLights: Opponent posts one hand on the mat — you can trap that arm AND foot on the same side
Opponent leans forward with their head over yours (weight shifted forward)
Opponent has both feet flat on the mat — NOT grapevined (no hooks)
Opponent is attempting a choke and momentarily not focused on their base
redLights: Opponent has both arms free and good wide base — nothing is trapped
Opponent is in high mount with grapevine hooks (very hard to bridge)
You've only trapped the arm but NOT the leg — they'll just post their foot to block
Opponent is sitting very upright with wide base on BOTH sides
steps: Trap their arm on one side: HUG their wrist/forearm tight to your chest. Overhook that arm so it can't escape.
Trap their foot on the SAME SIDE: step your foot over their ankle, pinning it to the mat. They now can't post arm or foot on that side.
Bridge EXPLOSIVELY: drive your hips straight UP toward the ceiling, then arc OVER your shoulder toward the trapped side. Imagine punching the ceiling with your hips.
Follow through the bridge momentum and roll them over. End in their closed guard on top. Immediately posture up and begin working a guard break.
complexpieces: If they resist the bridge, immediately switch to elbow-knee escape on the OPPOSITE side
Use the bridge to create space, then transition to guard recovery instead of the full reversal
Chain: bridge one direction (they resist), immediately bridge to the other side
simplifications: Practice bridging alone (explosive hip bridge with shoulder turn) as a solo drill
Practice trapping just the arm, then just the foot — separately
Start with partner giving zero resistance to learn the exact direction of the bridge

techniques.name: Elbow-Knee Escape (Shrimp)
techniques.goal: Create space under mount by shrimping your hips away and inserting your knee/leg inside to recover guard. A technique-based escape using hip movement, not strength.

initialPosition.body: On back underneath mount. Elbows tight to your body. Hands framing on opponent's hips or knees.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands framing on their hips, knees, or belt — pushing them toward your feet. Elbows stay tight, never flaring out.
initialPosition.legs: Feet flat on the mat, knees up, ready to bridge slightly then shrimp.
initialPosition.hips: Ready to turn to one side and shrimp (hip escape) away from opponent.

greenLights: Opponent is sitting heavy but you can frame on their hips/knees
You have enough space to turn to your side after a small bump
Opponent is not attacking a submission — you have a moment to work
You can get at least one elbow to the mat to turn to your side
redLights: Opponent has grapevine hooks locked in — hip movement is severely limited
Opponent is actively choking you — defend the choke FIRST, escape second
You have no frames and opponent has both arms controlling your upper body
Opponent is in very high mount near your head (bridge first to lower them before shrimping)
steps: Frame both hands on opponent's near-side hip/knee. Bump with a small bridge to create space and TURN to your side, facing away from opponent.
SHRIMP your hips AWAY from them (toward the direction you're facing), creating space between your hip and their leg.
Slide your near-side knee INTO the space between you and them. Wedge your knee in as a frame/shield.
Continue shrimping and working your other leg inside. Recover to full guard or half guard. Re-establish grips immediately.
complexpieces: Chain: bridge one way (upa attempt), when they post, immediately shrimp the OTHER way
Transition to deep half guard instead of full guard for immediate sweep options
From recovered half guard, immediately fight for the underhook to sweep
simplifications: Practice shrimping/hip escapes as a solo drill extensively (this is the #1 movement to drill)
Drill only the knee insertion step against a stationary partner
Practice the full escape with partner offering very light resistance to learn the movement path

positions.name: Side Control
positions.description: Top player controls opponent from the side with chest-to-chest pressure. Strong dominant position with many submission and transition options.

techniques.name: Americana from Side Control
techniques.goal: Submit opponent with an Americana shoulder lock from side control, using the same figure-four mechanics as from mount — pin the wrist, rotate the arm in an arc.

initialPosition.body: In side control on top. Chest heavy on opponent's chest, head low. Strong crossface established.
initialPosition.grips: Crossface arm behind their head initially, then transitions: same-side hand pins opponent's near wrist to the mat beside their head. Other hand threads under their arm for the figure-four grip.
initialPosition.legs: Sprawled back with hips heavy and low. Near knee can be tight against their hip, far leg sprawled back for base.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy pressure driving INTO opponent's side. Don't leave space.

greenLights: Opponent is flat on their back with near-side arm exposed (not tucked)
Opponent pushes against your neck/chin with their near-side arm — arm is accessible
You have strong crossface and hip control already established
Opponent is not actively framing or shrimping — they're relatively static
redLights: Opponent has strong frames with elbows tight to their body
Opponent is actively shrimping and fighting to recover guard
Their near-side arm is completely tucked and inaccessible
You don't have stable side control yet — establish the position first
steps: From side control, switch your crossface hand to PIN opponent's near-side wrist to the mat beside their head.
Slide your other hand UNDER their arm and grab your own wrist (figure-four grip). Their elbow should be bent at approximately 90°.
Keep the elbow pinned DOWN using your chest/shoulder weight. Slowly slide their wrist toward their hip — painting an arc along the mat.
Apply smooth, controlled pressure. The shoulder joint rotation against the pinned elbow creates the submission.
complexpieces: If they straighten their arm to defend, transition to armbar from side control
If they turn to their side, the grip angle shifts — switch to kimura
Chain with north-south: transition to north-south position for a north-south kimura
simplifications: Practice the figure-four grip from a static side control position
Drill the wrist-to-hip arc to learn the exact direction of pressure
Focus on maintaining heavy side control before adding any submission

techniques.name: Kimura from Side Control
techniques.goal: Submit opponent with a kimura shoulder lock by controlling their arm in a figure-four and rotating their wrist behind their back against the shoulder joint.

initialPosition.body: In side control, transitioning to face opponent's legs (north-south direction). Weight heavy on their upper body.
initialPosition.grips: Kimura grip (figure-four): one hand grabs opponent's wrist, other hand reaches OVER their arm and grabs your own wrist. Both hands lock together.
initialPosition.legs: Near knee tight against opponent's body, far leg posted for base. Transitioning to step over their head toward north-south.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy, driving down to keep their arm pinned and prevent escape.

greenLights: Opponent reaches across with their near arm (trying to push you or frame on you)
Opponent turns to their side, exposing their arm behind them
You've established strong cross-body control and they're relatively flat
Their near arm is separated from their body — accessible for the grip
redLights: Opponent's arm is completely tight to their body, elbow locked at their side
They're gripping their own belt or pants to defend (grip fight first)
You don't have your hips blocking their hip movement — they can escape
Opponent is actively escaping to their knees or recovering guard
steps: From side control, grab opponent's near-side wrist with your hip-side hand. Release crossface and reach OVER their arm to grab your own wrist — figure-four kimura grip.
Step over their head toward north-south for better leverage. Peel their arm off the mat by pulling their elbow toward you.
Keep their elbow bent at 90°. Pin their forearm to the mat using your body weight pressing down.
Rotate their wrist toward their back (like pointing their hand at the ceiling, then toward their spine). Apply smooth, rotational pressure on the shoulder.
complexpieces: If they roll to escape, follow them to mount with the kimura grip still secured — finish from mount
Transition to armbar if they straighten the arm to defend
Use the kimura grip to take the back: roll them over and insert your hooks as they turn
simplifications: Practice the kimura figure-four grip in isolation until it's automatic
Drill the north-south step-over for improved angle without finishing the sub
Start with their arm already separated from their body to focus on finishing mechanics

techniques.name: Shrimp to Guard Recovery
techniques.goal: Escape from bottom side control by framing, shrimping your hips away, and inserting your knee to recover guard position.

initialPosition.body: On your back under side control. Turned slightly to face opponent. Elbows inside — never let arms get trapped.
initialPosition.grips: Near-side forearm frames on their neck/jaw line (preventing crossface pressure). Far-side hand frames on their hip, pushing them away.
initialPosition.legs: Feet flat on the mat, knees up, ready to bridge then shrimp.
initialPosition.hips: Ready to escape AWAY from opponent (toward the direction you're facing).

greenLights: You have a solid frame on their hip and neck/jaw — they're not crushing you
Opponent hasn't settled heavy — you still have hip mobility
Your arms are inside and not trapped under their body
You can turn to your side and face them
redLights: Opponent has a tight crossface and you can't get your forearm frame on their neck
Your near-side arm is trapped under their body — free it first
Opponent has kesa gatame (head and arm control) — need a different escape specific to kesa
You're completely flat with no frames established — build frames first before shrimping
steps: Establish frames: near-side forearm across their throat/jaw, far-side hand pushing their hip. Get your elbows INSIDE — don't let them pin your arms.
Bump/bridge INTO them slightly to create a moment of space, then immediately SHRIMP your hips AWAY (toward the direction you're facing).
Get your near-side knee INSIDE the space between you and them. Use your knee as a shield and wedge.
Continue shrimping until you get your far leg inside too. Recover to full guard (lock ankles), half guard, or butterfly guard. Establish grips immediately.
complexpieces: If they block the knee from coming in, shrimp AGAIN with another bump and try a different angle
Transition to an underhook escape if the guard recovery is blocked
Recover to butterfly guard instead of closed guard — immediately look for butterfly sweep
simplifications: Solo drill: practice the shrimp/hip escape motion on the mat (50 reps each side daily)
Drill only getting the near-side knee inside vs light resistance
Start with opponent being static in side control to learn the movement path

positions.name: Back Control
positions.description: The highest-scoring position in BJJ. Attacker controls opponent from behind with hooks or body triangle. The dominant attacking position in the sport.

techniques.name: Rear Naked Choke (RNC)
techniques.goal: Choke opponent from back control using your arms in a figure-four around their neck. The forearm and bicep compress both carotid arteries — a blood choke.

initialPosition.body: Behind opponent with your chest on their back. Seatbelt control (one arm over shoulder, one under armpit).
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm slides under their chin — forearm across the front of their throat, crook of elbow at center. Other hand grabs your own bicep (figure-four). Free hand goes behind their head.
initialPosition.legs: Both hooks in (feet inserted inside opponent's thighs, heels digging in) OR body triangle locked around their waist.
initialPosition.hips: Tight against their back, slightly offset to the choking arm side for angle.

greenLights: You have solid back control with hooks or body triangle — position is established
Opponent's chin is exposed and not tucked (you can slide your arm under)
You already have one arm around their neck or seatbelt control
Opponent is hand-fighting but you can work around their defending hands
redLights: Opponent has two hands firmly grip-fighting your choking arm with strong defense
You have no hooks or body triangle — back control isn't established yet
Opponent is about to turn into you to make it a guard situation
Your choking arm is too low (across their chest, not their neck)
steps: From back control with hooks in, fight for your choking arm to get UNDER their chin. Use your other hand to peel down their defending hands or use hand-fighting setups.
Slide your forearm under their chin — position the crook of your elbow directly under the center of their chin. Forearm on one side of the neck, bicep on the other.
Grab your own bicep with the choking hand (figure-four lock). Place your free hand behind their head — palm on the back of their skull.
SQUEEZE by expanding your chest, pulling elbows together, and pressing the back of their head forward with your free hand. Both carotid arteries are compressed.
complexpieces: If they defend their neck, switch to arm triangle, armbar from back, or bow and arrow choke
Short choke: if you can't get under the chin, apply pressure on the jawline to force the chin up
Use collar choke setups (in gi) to force reactions that expose the neck for the RNC
simplifications: Practice the figure-four grip extensively (hand-to-bicep, hand-behind-head)
Drill hand fighting to get under the chin against a partner who actively defends
Focus first on maintaining back control for 2 minutes before attacking any choke

techniques.name: Bow and Arrow Choke
techniques.goal: Choke opponent from back control using their own collar and a pants grip to stretch them out like drawing a bow. Extremely powerful gi choke.

initialPosition.body: Behind opponent in back control. Transitioning to fall to the side at an angle, pulling opponent onto you.
initialPosition.grips: Cross collar grip: top hand feeds deep across their neck gripping their far-side collar (4 fingers inside, knuckles against neck). Bottom hand grabs their near-side pant leg at the knee.
initialPosition.legs: Top hook controls opponent's hip. Bottom leg posts on the mat for leverage and drive.
initialPosition.hips: Falling to the side (toward the collar grip direction), creating the stretching angle.

greenLights: You have back control and a deep collar grip established (past the tag on the collar)
You can reach and grip their far-side pant leg at the knee
Opponent is defending the RNC — bow and arrow is the backup plan
You have strong seatbelt control transitioning to the collar grip
redLights: Back control isn't established (no hooks or body triangle)
Can't reach their pants — they're curled up in a tight ball
Collar grip is too shallow (not deep enough past the neck area)
Opponent has already turned to face you — the back position is lost
steps: From back control, feed your top arm DEEP across their neck and grip their far-side collar (4 fingers inside). Deep grip is crucial — knuckles against their neck.
With your bottom hand, reach down and grab their near-side pant leg at the knee. Top hook stays controlling their hip.
FALL to the side (toward the collar grip side), extending their body — pull the collar one direction, pull the pant leg the other, like drawing a bow.
Squeeze by pulling the collar tight (elbow toward your OWN ribs) while extending their leg away. The collar tightens across the arteries for the choke.
complexpieces: If you can't reach pants, use their belt or control the leg with your hook while choking with just the collar
Chain with armbar: if they defend by pulling the collar away, their arm is exposed
Transition from failed RNC: when they defend the RNC hand, swim it to their collar instead
simplifications: Practice getting a deep collar grip from behind repeatedly — depth is everything
Focus on the falling and angling motion without the choke to understand body positioning
Use a dummy or very cooperative partner to learn the pulling/stretching mechanics

techniques.name: Back Escape to Guard
techniques.goal: Escape opponent's back control and recover to a guard position. Priority is defending the neck first, then escaping the position systematically.

initialPosition.body: Opponent has your back. Your back is against their chest. Chin is tucked, shoulders are raised to protect neck.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands fighting on opponent's choking arm: one hand controls their wrist, other hand controls the crook of their elbow — two-on-one grip defense.
initialPosition.legs: Fighting their hooks — working to clear the bottom hook first.
initialPosition.hips: Preparing to scoot DOWN toward the bottom hook side.

greenLights: You've defended the choke (two hands controlling their choking arm securely)
Opponent's bottom hook is lighter or you can trap it with your legs
You can scoot your hips toward the bottom hook side
You have a solid grip on their choking arm preventing collar or RNC entry
redLights: Opponent has a body triangle locked — address the body triangle lock FIRST before escaping
You're currently being choked — defend the neck IMMEDIATELY, escape later
You're trying to escape toward the top hook side (wrong direction — exposes your back more)
Opponent has a deep seatbelt and you can't access their arms at all
steps: IMMEDIATELY defend the neck with both hands: control their choking arm with two-on-one grip (one hand on their wrist, one hand on their elbow). Chin tucked, shoulders raised.
Scoot your hips DOWN toward their bottom hook side. Pin their bottom hook to the mat by walking your hips over it.
Slide over their trapped bottom leg, turning INTO them. Your back presses to the mat on the side of the trapped hook.
Continue turning until you FACE them, recovering into their closed guard or half guard. Immediately establish defensive grips and recover posture.
complexpieces: When clearing the hooks, look for a leg lock entry as a surprise counterattack
Use the momentum of the escape to go directly to top position instead of guard
If opponent has body triangle: extend the trapped leg, scoot hips down, pop the triangle lock first
simplifications: Drill neck defense first: practice controlling both hands on the choking arm for 30 seconds
Practice just the hip-scoot direction — DOWN toward bottom hook — as a movement drill
Work the escape with partner only using hooks (no grips or choke attempts) to learn the movement

positions.name: Open Guard
positions.description: Guard player's legs are not locked around opponent. Includes seated guard, supine guard, and all variations. Very dynamic position with multiple grip combinations.

techniques.name: Tripod Sweep
techniques.goal: Sweep a standing opponent by simultaneously pushing their hip, pulling both ankles, and hooking behind one knee — collapsing them like a tripod losing a leg.

initialPosition.body: On your back in open guard. Opponent is standing above you. Core engaged, hips slightly elevated.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's ankles or pant cuffs. One foot planted on their hip (same side as one grip) pushing them away.
initialPosition.legs: One foot on their hip, pushing and controlling distance. Other foot hooks behind their far-side knee or calf from the outside.
initialPosition.hips: Slightly elevated, engaged core. Not flat and passive.

greenLights: Opponent is standing with both feet within reach of your grips
You can grip both of their ankles or pant cuffs solidly
Opponent's weight is evenly distributed or slightly backward
Opponent is focused on gripping you and not defending their feet
redLights: Opponent has a very staggered stance with one foot far back — can't reach both ankles
Opponent is crouched very low with an extremely wide base
You can't get a grip on either of their legs (strong grip fighting)
Opponent is actively grabbing your legs to initiate their own pass
steps: Grip both of opponent's ankles or pant cuffs with your hands. Place one foot on their hip (same side as one grip) to control distance.
Place your other foot behind their far knee — hooking behind the knee or calf from the outside.
SIMULTANEOUSLY: PUSH their hip with your foot, PULL their ankles toward you with your hands, and SWEEP behind their knee to kick it out. All three forces at once.
Opponent falls backward. IMMEDIATELY come up on top — don't stay on your back. Technical stand-up or follow into a passing position.
complexpieces: If they step one leg back, transition to single leg X-guard or De La Riva
Chain with sickle sweep: if they step over the hooking foot, switch to sickle sweep (same grips, different leg configuration)
Follow the sweep immediately with a toreando or leg drag pass
simplifications: Practice the three simultaneous forces (push-pull-sweep) without the hook first — just push hip + pull ankles
Drill standing up rapidly after a sweep (technical stand-up from the ground)
Start with partner standing stationary to learn grip placement and foot positions

techniques.name: Toreando Pass (vs Open Guard)
techniques.goal: Pass opponent's open guard by controlling both legs and explosively moving laterally (like a bullfighter) past their legs into side control.

initialPosition.body: Standing over opponent who is in open guard. Athletic, slightly crouched stance. Ready to explode laterally.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's pants at the knees, or controlling their shins/ankles. From the outside of their legs.
initialPosition.legs: Standing, feet shoulder-width, slightly staggered for explosive lateral movement.
initialPosition.hips: Athletic stance — hips low, loaded, ready to explode sideways.

greenLights: Opponent is playing seated or supine open guard without strong upper body grips on you
You have solid control of both their legs/pants
Opponent's grips on you are broken or minimal (no collar, no sleeves)
Opponent's legs are relatively relaxed — you can redirect them
redLights: Opponent has strong collar AND sleeve grips (spider guard, lasso guard)
Opponent has a hook on your leg (De La Riva, X-guard) — strip hooks first
You don't have a grip on at least one of their legs (need control of both to pass)
Opponent is actively inverting or re-guarding as you move
steps: Grip both of opponent's pants at the knees or control their ankles. Maintain standing posture above them.
Push one leg DOWN to the mat (pinning it), while directing their other leg across their body to the far side. Control the direction of their hips.
EXPLODE laterally — move your hips fast to the side, like a bullfighter stepping past the bull. Keep pressure on their legs to prevent reguarding.
Landing in side control: near arm drives under their head (crossface), far arm under their hip. Drop chest-to-chest pressure, sprawl legs back.
complexpieces: Fake one direction then switch sides when they react — toreando switch
If they play knee shield, chain into knee slice pass
Combine with leg drag: instead of directing the leg across, drag it over your thigh — even tighter pass
simplifications: Practice the lateral footwork pattern as a solo agility drill
Work on grip fighting: practice breaking their sleeve and collar grips quickly first
Drill the toreando from a static open guard to learn the correct passing angle

positions.name: Spider Guard
positions.description: Gi-based open guard where bottom player controls opponent's sleeves while placing feet on biceps. Extending and retracting legs controls distance, posture, and balance for sweeps and submissions.

techniques.name: Spider Guard Sweep
techniques.goal: Push one bicep while pulling the sleeve and angling the hips to sweep the opponent sideways using spider guard control.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in spider guard. Hips mobile and slightly off the mat, angled toward the sweeping direction.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's sleeves. Feet on their biceps with toes pointing outward, creating tension.
initialPosition.legs: One leg extends to push their bicep while the other retracts to pull. Alternating push-pull creates off-balance.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the sweep direction, slightly elevated off the mat for mobility.

greenLights: You have strong double sleeve grips with feet on both biceps
Opponent's weight is shifted to one side or their base is narrow
Opponent is reaching forward trying to break your grips — weight is committed
You've successfully pushed one arm across their body, compromising their posture
redLights: Opponent has broken one of your sleeve grips — regrip before sweeping
Opponent is stacking you by driving forward with heavy pressure
Your feet have slipped off their biceps — re-establish hooks first
Opponent has widened their base significantly on both sides
steps: From spider guard with double sleeve grips and feet on biceps, identify which direction to sweep based on opponent's weight distribution.
PUSH one bicep away with your foot while PULLING the opposite sleeve across their body. Angle your hips toward the sweep direction.
Use the push-pull asymmetry to break their base — their weight shifts sideways as you redirect their arm.
Follow through the sweep, coming up to top position. Maintain at least one sleeve grip throughout the transition.
complexpieces: Chain with triangle: if they resist the sweep by posturing, throw your leg over their shoulder for a triangle
If they step one leg forward to resist, transition to De La Riva hook on that leg
Alternate sweep directions: fake one way, then sweep the opposite when they shift weight
simplifications: Practice the push-pull leg motion with a cooperative partner — focus on the timing of simultaneous push and pull
Drill hip angle changes while maintaining feet on biceps as a movement drill
Start with a static partner to learn the correct sweeping angle before adding resistance

techniques.name: Balloon Sweep
techniques.goal: Use sleeve grips and leg extension to elevate the opponent and sweep them over your head from spider guard.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in spider guard. Scooting hips under opponent to load their weight onto your feet.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's sleeves tightly. Pulling them forward to commit their weight over you.
initialPosition.legs: Feet on opponent's biceps or transitioning to their hips/stomach for the elevation. Legs coiled and ready to extend.
initialPosition.hips: Scooted underneath opponent, positioned to elevate them overhead with leg extension.

greenLights: Opponent is leaning forward with their weight committed over you
You've loaded their weight onto your feet successfully
Strong double sleeve grips are maintained — they can't post their hands
Opponent's hips are over your center of gravity
redLights: Opponent is sitting back with hips away — can't load their weight onto your feet
You've lost a sleeve grip — they'll post a hand and block the sweep
Opponent has very wide base and low center of gravity
Your hips aren't underneath them — not enough elevation angle
steps: From spider guard, pull opponent forward with double sleeve grips to commit their weight over you. Scoot your hips under them.
Transition your feet from biceps to their hips or stomach area to create a platform for elevation.
EXTEND your legs explosively while PULLING the sleeves overhead. Guide them over your head in a controlled arc.
Follow through the sweep, rolling backward and coming up on top. Maintain sleeve control throughout the transition.
complexpieces: If they resist the overhead motion, redirect to a lateral spider sweep
Chain with overhead sweep: same setup, different leg angle for the elevation
Use single spider (one foot on bicep, one on hip) for a more controlled elevation
simplifications: Practice with a lightweight or cooperative partner to learn the elevation mechanics
Drill the hip scoot underneath to load their weight — the setup is the hardest part
Focus on the timing of simultaneous leg extension and sleeve pull

techniques.name: Overhead Sweep (Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Pull the opponent forward and extend your legs to flip them over your head from spider guard, using the sleeve grips to prevent posting.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in spider guard. Pulling opponent forward aggressively to break their posture over you.
initialPosition.grips: Double sleeve grips, pulling opponent's arms forward and down. Preventing them from posting hands on the mat.
initialPosition.legs: Feet on biceps transitioning to hips for the flip. Legs coiled, ready for explosive upward extension.
initialPosition.hips: Under opponent's center of gravity, positioned to flip them overhead.

greenLights: Opponent's posture is broken forward — weight is over you
You have strong sleeve control on both arms
Opponent is stepping forward or lunging to pass — momentum carries them over
You can plant your feet on their hips or stomach for the elevation
redLights: Opponent has strong upright posture with hips back
You've lost one or both sleeve grips
Opponent is stepping laterally to avoid going overhead
Your legs are too extended to generate the flip — need to coil first
steps: From spider guard, aggressively PULL both sleeves forward and down, breaking opponent's posture over you.
As they stumble forward, plant your feet on their hips. Scoot your hips under them to position for the flip.
EXTEND legs explosively upward and overhead while pulling the sleeves past your head. Launch them over you.
Roll through to top position as they land behind you. Keep at least one sleeve grip for immediate control.
complexpieces: If they base and resist mid-flip, transition to armbar on an extended arm
Chain with triangle: if they posture during the attempt, throw your leg over for triangle
Fake the overhead sweep to pull them forward, then redirect to a lateral sweep
simplifications: Practice the foot-on-hip elevation with a cooperative partner
Drill the sleeve pull and hip scoot as separate movements first
Start with partner already leaning forward to focus on the flip mechanics

techniques.name: Tripod Sweep (Spider Setup)
techniques.goal: From spider guard control, place a foot on the hip and another behind the ankle to create a tripod structure that collapses their base for the sweep.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back, transitioning from full spider guard to a tripod configuration. Body angled toward the sweeping side.
initialPosition.grips: One hand maintains sleeve grip. Other hand grabs opponent's far ankle or pant leg at the heel.
initialPosition.legs: One foot pushes against opponent's near-side hip. Other foot hooks behind their far ankle from the outside.
initialPosition.hips: On the mat, angled slightly toward the sweep direction.

greenLights: You can reach opponent's far ankle with your hand while maintaining a sleeve grip
Opponent is standing with feet relatively close together
Your foot is solidly on their hip with good pushing power
Opponent is focused on passing and not defending their ankles
redLights: Opponent has very wide stance — hard to reach far ankle
You've lost your sleeve grip and can't prevent them from posting
Opponent is kneeling — tripod sweep requires them to be standing or in combat base
Your foot hook behind their ankle has slipped off
steps: From spider guard, release one foot from their bicep and place it on their near-side hip. Maintain the sleeve grip on that side.
Reach with your free hand and grab their far-side ankle or pant cuff at the heel. Hook your other foot behind that same ankle from the outside.
Simultaneously PUSH on their hip with your foot while PULLING the sleeve and SWEEPING the ankle with your foot hook. All three forces work together.
Their base collapses and they fall backward or sideways. Come up to top position immediately.
complexpieces: If they step over your hooking foot, transition to De La Riva guard on that leg
Chain with sickle sweep: if the tripod is blocked, switch the hooking leg to a sickle motion across both ankles
Fake the tripod sweep to pull them forward, then switch to an overhead sweep
simplifications: Practice the three-point structure (hand on ankle, foot on hip, foot behind ankle) statically
Drill the push-pull-sweep timing with a standing partner giving light resistance
Master the ankle grip first — the most common failure point is losing foot/hand contact on the ankle

techniques.name: Lasso Sweep (Transition from Spider)
techniques.goal: Transition from spider guard into lasso guard by wrapping your leg around opponent's arm, then use rotational pressure to sweep them.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back, transitioning from spider to lasso. One leg wraps OVER and AROUND opponent's arm from the outside.
initialPosition.grips: Same-side sleeve grip on the lasso side (essential to keep the arm trapped). Other hand grips collar, far sleeve, or pant leg.
initialPosition.legs: Lasso leg: shin wraps over and around opponent's bicep from outside, foot threads inside near their armpit. Other leg: foot on hip or free for sweeping.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the lasso side, creating rotational leverage for the sweep.

greenLights: You've wrapped your shin over and around their arm with the lasso — arm is fully trapped
Sleeve grip on the lasso side is solid
Opponent can't retract their arm from the lasso wrap
You have a secondary grip (collar, sleeve, or pants) for sweep direction
redLights: Opponent has pulled their arm free from the lasso — regrip and re-wrap
Your sleeve grip is broken on the lasso side — the wrap loses effectiveness
Opponent is stepping back and creating distance to unwrap the lasso
Opponent has strong posture and both arms free
steps: From spider guard, feed one foot over and around opponent's arm — wrap your shin over the bicep from outside in, threading your foot near their armpit. Maintain the sleeve grip.
Your lasso leg now traps their arm. Grab their collar, far sleeve, or far pant leg with your free hand.
Create rotational force: PULL the trapped arm across their body while using your hips and lasso leg to rotate them. Push with your free foot on their hip.
Sweep them laterally or backward using the rotational pressure. Come up to top position, maintaining the lasso control during transition.
complexpieces: If they resist the sweep, use the lasso to set up omoplata or triangle
Deep lasso variation: thread the foot all the way to their back for maximum control
Chain between lasso and full spider — transition back and forth to keep them reacting
simplifications: Practice the leg wrap (over and around the arm) as an isolated movement drill
Drill the lasso entry from spider guard without finishing the sweep
Focus on maintaining the sleeve grip — without it, the lasso unravels

techniques.name: De La Riva Transition Sweep (from Spider)
techniques.goal: Transition one leg from spider guard to a De La Riva hook on opponent's lead leg, then attack sweeping angles from the hybrid position.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back, transitioning from spider guard. One foot drops from bicep to hook around opponent's lead leg.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve grip maintained on the DLR side. Other hand grips collar, far sleeve, or opponent's far ankle.
initialPosition.legs: One leg: DLR hook around opponent's lead leg (shin behind their calf, foot hooking inside thigh). Other leg: remains on bicep in spider position or pushes on hip.
initialPosition.hips: Angled away from the hooked leg, creating the DLR sweeping angle.

greenLights: Opponent steps one leg forward — the lead leg is accessible for the DLR hook
You can maintain the sleeve grip while transitioning the hook
Opponent is standing or in combat base — DLR is most effective here
Your spider control has disrupted their posture and they're reacting
redLights: Opponent is kneeling with no lead leg exposed for the hook
You've lost both sleeve grips during the transition
Opponent immediately backsteps when they feel the DLR hook
You can't maintain the hybrid position — pick one guard or the other
steps: From spider guard, when opponent steps a leg forward, release that foot from their bicep and hook it BEHIND their lead calf — DLR hook (shin behind calf, foot hooks inner thigh).
Maintain your same-side sleeve grip. Use your other hand to grab their far ankle, collar, or belt.
Use the DLR hook to off-balance: pull their lead leg forward while pushing with your spider foot on the other bicep or their hip.
Sweep them to the side or take the back as their base is disrupted. Follow through to top position or back control.
complexpieces: From the hybrid spider-DLR, transition to berimbolo for a back take
If sweep is blocked, transition to full DLR guard and attack from there
Use the spider-DLR combination to set up X-guard entry underneath them
simplifications: Practice the DLR hook insertion from spider guard as a transition drill
Drill the hybrid position hold: one foot spider, one foot DLR — maintain for 30 seconds
Focus on maintaining sleeve grips throughout the transition — grips make or break this technique

techniques.name: Triangle Choke (from Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Use spider guard sleeve control and leg movement to trap the opponent's neck and one arm in a triangle choke configuration.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in spider guard. Hips angled to create the arm-in, arm-out situation needed for the triangle.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve grips controlling both arms. One sleeve is pulled ACROSS their body while the other arm is pushed away — creating the arm-in, arm-out.
initialPosition.legs: One foot drops from bicep to hip to create the angle. Other leg swings HIGH over opponent's shoulder and behind their neck.
initialPosition.hips: Scooted to one side for the angle needed to throw the leg over their shoulder.

greenLights: You've created an arm-in, arm-out situation with your spider grips
One arm is pulled across their body while the other is pushed wide
Opponent's posture is broken forward — close enough for your leg to reach their head
You have the angle to throw your leg over their shoulder
redLights: Both of opponent's arms are equally extended — no arm-in, arm-out yet
Opponent has strong upright posture — too far away to reach with your leg
You've lost a sleeve grip — can't control the arm positioning
Opponent is actively backing away and creating distance
steps: From spider guard, pull one sleeve ACROSS their body (this is the arm-in). Push the other arm away with your foot on their bicep (arm-out).
Drop the foot from the arm-in side to their hip. Use the angle to throw your other leg HIGH over their shoulder, behind their neck.
Lock the triangle: back of your knee over your opposite ankle (figure-four). Angle your body 30° to the side for optimal choke geometry.
Squeeze knees together, pull their head DOWN, and lift your hips. The trapped arm's shoulder compresses one carotid, your thigh compresses the other.
complexpieces: If they posture inside the triangle, switch to armbar on the trapped arm
If they stack you, transition to omoplata by releasing the lock and controlling the shoulder
Set up the triangle by faking a sweep first — when they resist, their arm positioning gives you the opening
simplifications: Practice the arm-in, arm-out creation from spider grips as a separate drill
Drill the leg-over-shoulder throw from spider position without locking the triangle
Focus first on the angle creation — hip scoot and foot on hip — before adding the triangle

techniques.name: Omoplata (from Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's shoulder when their posture breaks during spider guard control, swinging your leg over their shoulder for the omoplata shoulder lock.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in spider guard, transitioning to perpendicular angle. Rotating body 90° relative to opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve grip on the arm being attacked, pulling it across your body. Other hand assists the rotation or controls their hip.
initialPosition.legs: Attacking leg releases from bicep and swings HIGH over opponent's shoulder. Other foot pushes on their hip for the pivot.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to create a perpendicular angle — similar setup to armbar but finishing with shoulder pressure.

greenLights: Opponent's posture is broken and their arm is extended forward
You've pulled one arm across your body with the sleeve grip
You can swing your leg over their shoulder from the spider position
Opponent is focused on defending the spider sweep and not watching the arm attack
redLights: Opponent has strong posture and you can't reach their shoulder with your leg
You've lost the sleeve grip on the attacking arm — can't control it
Opponent is backing away creating too much distance
Opponent is already rolling forward to escape before you establish control
steps: From spider guard, pull one sleeve across your body. Drop that foot from their bicep and push on their hip with your other foot for the pivot.
Swing your attacking leg HIGH over their shoulder, draping it across their back. Your leg traps their arm against your body.
Sit up and control their hip or belt to prevent the forward roll escape. Establish the perpendicular angle.
Lean forward, driving their arm behind their back using your leg pressure on their shoulder. Apply smooth rotational pressure for the submission.
complexpieces: If they roll forward to escape, follow the roll and sweep to top position
Chain from triangle: if your triangle attempt fails, redirect the leg for omoplata
Set up a wrist lock on the trapped arm while maintaining the omoplata position
simplifications: Practice the leg swing from spider position over partner's shoulder as a movement drill
Drill sitting up and controlling the hip — most common mistake is staying flat on your back
Break into phases: (1) pull arm across, (2) pivot and swing leg, (3) sit up and control, (4) finish

techniques.name: Armbar (from Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Swing your leg over the opponent's arm when they attempt to posture or defend in spider guard, isolating the arm for a hyperextension submission.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in spider guard. Hips angled to create a perpendicular angle to opponent's body for the armbar.
initialPosition.grips: Wrist or sleeve grip on the arm being attacked. Other hand controls their posture (collar, head, or other sleeve).
initialPosition.legs: One foot pushes on their hip for the pivot. Other leg swings over their face/head for the armbar clamp. Both feet were originally on biceps.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to perpendicular angle, scooted to the side for the armbar geometry.

greenLights: Opponent reaches forward trying to break your spider grips — arm is extended and exposed
You've controlled one wrist/sleeve and can pivot your hips for the armbar angle
Opponent's posture is broken and they're leaning forward
One arm is isolated while the other is controlled or pushed away
redLights: Both of opponent's arms are retracted tight to their body
Opponent has strong upright posture with hips back — too far for your legs
You've lost sleeve control — they'll pull the arm out during the spin
Opponent is stacking you, driving hips over your head
steps: From spider guard, isolate one arm by maintaining a tight wrist/sleeve grip. Push on their hip with one foot for the pivot.
Swing your other leg HIGH over their head/face, clamping down across their neck. Pivot your hips to create a perpendicular angle.
Clamp BOTH legs tight — one over their face, one across their torso. Their thumb points UP (arm extended, elbow joint facing your hips).
