Squeeze knees together, pinch tight, and lift your hips UP against their elbow for the submission. Control at the wrist while hips provide the breaking pressure.
complexpieces: If they stack you, transition to triangle or omoplata — the leg is already over their shoulder
Chain with triangle: if they pull arm out during the armbar, lock up the triangle
Set up from a sweep attempt: when they base with an extended arm to resist, catch the armbar
simplifications: Practice the hip pivot from spider guard to the perpendicular armbar angle as a movement drill
Drill the leg-over-face clamp from spider position without finishing the armbar
Break into two parts: (1) angle creation and leg placement, (2) finishing mechanics with hip lift

techniques.name: Triangle to Armbar Combo (from Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Attempt a triangle choke from spider guard, and when the opponent defends by posturing or pulling their arm, transition immediately to an armbar on the trapped arm.

initialPosition.body: In the triangle position from spider guard. Legs locked in figure-four around opponent's neck and one arm. Ready to transition based on their defense.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling the trapped arm at the wrist. Other hand pulling their head down or controlling their posture.
initialPosition.legs: Triangle locked: back of knee over opposite ankle (figure-four) around their neck and trapped arm. Ready to unlock and switch to armbar clamp.
initialPosition.hips: Angled to one side for the triangle. Ready to pivot further for the armbar transition.

greenLights: You have the triangle locked but opponent is defending by posturing or tucking their chin
The trapped arm is still accessible for an armbar attack
Opponent is focused entirely on escaping the triangle — not defending the arm
You can control their wrist while unlocking your legs to transition
redLights: Triangle is very tight and opponent is close to tapping — finish it instead of switching
You've lost control of the trapped arm — can't transition to armbar
Opponent has already escaped the triangle — no arm to attack
Your legs are fatigued and can't maintain the clamp for either submission
steps: From the triangle position (locked from spider guard setup), control the trapped arm at the wrist. If opponent is defending the choke effectively, prepare to switch.
Unlock the triangle by releasing the figure-four. Keep the leg over their head/face in place and swing the other leg across their chest.
Pivot your hips for the armbar angle. Clamp BOTH legs tight — one over their face, one across their torso. Keep the wrist controlled with their thumb pointing up.
Squeeze knees together and lift your hips UP against their elbow. The transition catches them off guard as they were focused on defending the triangle.
complexpieces: If they defend the armbar by bending the arm, lock up the triangle again — chain back and forth
Add omoplata to the chain: triangle → armbar → omoplata creates a three-way submission loop
Use the threat of the triangle to force them to extend the arm, making the armbar easier
simplifications: Practice the triangle-to-armbar transition slowly with a cooperative partner
Drill just the leg switch: from triangle legs to armbar legs, repeatedly
Focus on maintaining wrist control throughout the transition — this is the most critical grip

techniques.name: Grip Break and Toreando Pass
techniques.goal: Break the spider guard sleeve grips and immediately circle around the legs using the toreando (bullfighter) pass to reach side control.

initialPosition.body: Standing in opponent's spider guard. They have feet on your biceps and sleeve grips. Posture upright, working to strip grips.
initialPosition.grips: Initially controlled by opponent. Working to break their sleeve grips by circling your wrists, pulling arms back, or using grip-breaking techniques.
initialPosition.legs: Standing with strong base. Ready to explode laterally once grips are broken.
initialPosition.hips: Upright and mobile, ready to change direction rapidly for the toreando pass.

greenLights: You've broken both of their sleeve grips — this is the trigger for the toreando
Opponent's feet have slipped off your biceps after the grip break
You can grab both of their pant legs at the knees or shins
Opponent is flat on their back after losing spider control
redLights: Opponent still has strong sleeve grips — break them before attempting to pass
Their feet are still actively on your biceps with tension
Opponent has transitioned to a different guard (lasso, DLR) as you strip grips
You've grabbed their legs but they've regripped your sleeve immediately
steps: Address the grips: circle your wrists outward, yank your arms back, or use two-on-one to strip their sleeve grips. Break both grips if possible.
The INSTANT grips are broken, grab both pant legs at the knees or shins. Push one leg DOWN and direct the other leg across.
Explode LATERALLY like a bullfighter — move your hips to the side, passing around their legs. Speed is critical before they regrip.
Drop into side control: crossface and hip control. Sprawl your legs back and drive chest-to-chest pressure.
complexpieces: If they regrip one sleeve during the pass, use the other hand to complete a single-side toreando
Chain with leg drag: as you pass, drag their top leg across your body for an even tighter pass
If they transition to lasso as you strip grips, strip the lasso by backsteping and circling the arm free
simplifications: Practice grip-breaking techniques in isolation — circle wrists, yank back, peel fingers
Drill the toreando footwork as a solo agility drill (lateral explosion)
Start from grips already broken to focus only on the toreando passing mechanics

techniques.name: Leg Drag Pass (vs Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Drag one of the opponent's legs across your body after breaking their spider guard hooks to pass to side control with tight hip-to-hip control.

initialPosition.body: Standing or kneeling in opponent's spider guard. Working to break their structure and control one leg for the drag.
initialPosition.grips: One hand grips opponent's pant leg at the ankle/shin. Other hand controls their collar, belt, or other pant leg.
initialPosition.legs: Strong base with hips close to opponent. Stepping to the drag side as you pull their leg across.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and to the side, pinning their dragged leg against your hip.

greenLights: You've broken their spider hooks and can grab a pant leg
You can drag their leg across your body and pin it against your hip
Opponent's guard is compromised after losing spider structure
You have a grip on their leg and can drive forward
redLights: Spider hooks are still active with strong sleeve grips — break structure first
Opponent is actively framing and preventing you from closing distance
They've regripped your sleeves and re-established spider guard
Opponent has transitioned to De La Riva or lasso as you attempt the drag
steps: Break spider guard structure: strip sleeve grips and clear their feet off your biceps.
Grab one pant leg at the ankle or shin. DRAG their leg across your body toward your opposite hip.
Step to the drag side and PIN their leg against your hip with your body. Drive your hips close to theirs — hip-to-hip contact.
Drop into side control on the drag side. Their hips are turned away, making reguarding very difficult. Secure crossface and chest pressure.
complexpieces: Chain with toreando: fake the toreando to one side, when they react, drag the leg the other way
If they try to reguard, immediately switch to knee slice on the dragged side
Use the leg drag position to set up a back take by stepping over
simplifications: Practice the leg drag motion (grabbing ankle, pulling across hip) as a partner drill
Drill the hip-to-hip pinning pressure from a static position
Start from spider guard already broken to focus only on the drag mechanics

techniques.name: Stack Pass (vs Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Drive the opponent's knees toward their chest to collapse the spider guard structure, then use stacking pressure to pass to side control.

initialPosition.body: Standing or kneeling in opponent's spider guard. Driving forward to collapse their leg structure.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's legs at the knees or thighs. Pushing their legs toward their chest. Hands may grip pants or hug inside the legs.
initialPosition.legs: Stepping forward with one foot up for drive. Other knee on the mat or both feet up driving forward.
initialPosition.hips: Driving FORWARD and DOWN, using bodyweight to stack their hips over their shoulders.

greenLights: You can drive forward and collapse their legs toward their chest
Opponent's feet have slipped off your biceps — spider hooks are neutralized
You have control of both legs and can stack their hips
Opponent is relatively flat and can't maintain distance with their legs
redLights: Spider hooks are still active — their feet on your biceps prevent you from closing distance
Opponent has strong frames keeping you away with extended legs
Opponent is very flexible and stacking has little effect on their mobility
You don't have control of both legs — one is free to frame or reguard
steps: Break the spider hooks by stripping grips and clearing feet off your biceps. Immediately close distance.
Drive FORWARD, pushing opponent's knees toward their chest. Shoulder pressure into their thighs, stacking their hips over their head.
Keep your head on one side (the side you'll pass to). Walk around toward that side, maintaining constant stacking pressure.
Once past the legs, release the stack and drop into side control. Secure crossface and hip control immediately.
complexpieces: If they frame with their arms during the stack, swim your arm through for an underhook
Chain with double under pass: if you get both arms under their legs during the stack
If they resist the stack, redirect to toreando — their legs are compressed and easy to redirect
simplifications: Practice the forward stacking drive against light resistance as a pressure drill
Drill walking around the stacked legs to learn the passing angle
Start from spider guard already broken to focus on the stacking and passing

techniques.name: Knee Slice Pass (vs Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Break spider guard sleeve grips and slice your knee through the middle, driving through their guard to reach side control.

initialPosition.body: Standing or kneeling, opponent's spider guard is partially broken. Transitioning to a knee slice position.
initialPosition.grips: Near arm: collar grip or crossface started. Far hand: controlling their pant leg or sleeve to prevent reguarding.
initialPosition.legs: Lead knee placed across opponent's thigh, pointing diagonally toward the mat. Rear leg posted back for base and forward drive.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy on the slicing knee, driving forward through the center of their guard.

greenLights: You've broken both sleeve grips and their feet are off your biceps
You can establish a crossface or collar grip to flatten them
Your knee can slot between their legs for the slice
Opponent is transitioning between guards and momentarily disorganized
redLights: Spider hooks are still active — break them before attempting knee slice
Opponent has transitioned to lasso or DLR — address those hooks first
You don't have head position (crossface) — vulnerable to being re-entangled
Opponent has strong frames preventing you from closing distance
steps: Break spider guard grips and clear their feet off your biceps. Immediately step into a knee slice position.
Secure crossface or collar grip on the passing side. Place your lead knee across their thigh, pointing diagonally toward the mat.
Drive your knee FORWARD through the center, slicing across their thigh. Maintain chest pressure and keep hips LOW throughout.
Clear past their legs and consolidate side control. Secure crossface and hip control. Don't let them reguard.
complexpieces: If they re-establish a hook during the slice, backstep to leg drag
Chain with toreando: fake the knee slice, when they defend, redirect to toreando
Use a collar grip to threaten a choke during the knee slice for added pressure
simplifications: Practice the knee slice motion from a static position against no resistance
Drill the grip break to knee slice transition as a seamless combination
Focus on maintaining heavy shoulder pressure through the entire slice — don't rise up

techniques.name: Double Under Stack Pass (vs Spider Guard)
techniques.goal: Secure both arms under opponent's legs and stack their hips to completely neutralize spider guard hooks and pass to side control.

initialPosition.body: Kneeling between opponent's legs. Spider hooks cleared or being cleared as you swim your arms under both legs.
initialPosition.grips: Both arms under opponent's legs — biceps under hamstrings. Hands clasp together (gable grip) behind their lower back or belt.
initialPosition.legs: Knees on the mat, tight base. One foot steps up when stacking begins.
initialPosition.hips: Driving FORWARD into opponent, stacking their hips over their shoulders.

greenLights: You've cleared their feet off your biceps — spider hooks are broken
You can swim both arms under their legs and clasp hands behind their back
Opponent is relatively flat and you can stack their hips
Their sleeve grips are broken — they can't re-establish spider control with your arms under their legs
redLights: Spider hooks are still active — your arms can't get under their legs
Opponent has strong collar grips that threaten chokes as you stack
You can only get under one leg (need both for double under)
Opponent is framing hard on your hips preventing you from getting close
steps: Break spider guard grips and clear feet off your biceps. Immediately drive forward and swim BOTH arms under opponent's legs.
Clasp your hands together (gable grip) behind their lower back or belt. Lock the grip tight.
Step one foot up and drive forward, stacking their hips OVER their shoulders. Your shoulder drives into their thighs. Keep your head on the passing side.
Walk around to the side your head is on, maintaining constant forward stacking pressure. Release the stack into side control with crossface and hip control.
complexpieces: If they grab your collar during the stack, transition to over-under pass by releasing one arm
Chain with toreando: if they kick out of the stack, switch to toreando immediately
São Paulo pass variation: use collar grip instead of clasping hands for collar-choke pressure during the pass
simplifications: Practice the double underhook entry against a static open guard
Drill the stacking motion and walk-around separately
Focus on the grip clasp behind their back — a loose grip means they'll escape the stack

positions.name: De La Riva
positions.description: Open guard position where the bottom player hooks the outside of the opponent's lead leg with their own leg while controlling grips to off-balance, create angles, and attack sweeps, back takes, or transitions to other guards.

techniques.name: Classic De La Riva Sweep
techniques.goal: Use the DLR hook and sleeve or collar grip to off-balance the opponent and sweep them to the side by pulling their base out from under them.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back with DLR hook established on opponent's lead leg. Hips angled away from the hooked leg.
initialPosition.grips: Far-side collar grip or sleeve grip to control their upper body. Other hand grips their far ankle or pant leg.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook: outside leg hooks behind opponent's lead calf, shin wrapping around, foot hooking inside thigh. Free leg: foot on their far hip or thigh for pushing.
initialPosition.hips: Angled away from the hooked leg, creating off-balance pressure. Hips slightly elevated off the mat.

greenLights: DLR hook is deep and tight around opponent's lead leg
You have a strong collar or sleeve grip controlling their posture
Opponent's weight is shifting forward over their lead leg
You can reach their far ankle to remove their posting base
redLights: Opponent has broken your DLR hook by backstepping
You've lost your upper body grip — no way to pull them off-balance
Opponent has a very wide base with weight on the back foot
Opponent is actively stripping your hook with their hands
steps: Establish DLR hook on opponent's lead leg — shin wraps behind their calf, foot hooks inside their thigh. Maintain constant tension in the hook.
Grip their far-side collar (pulling them forward and down) and their far ankle or pant cuff with your other hand.
PULL them forward with the collar grip while simultaneously pulling their far ankle toward you. Push their hooked leg with your DLR hook in the opposite direction.
Their base collapses sideways. Come up to top position maintaining grips. Establish side control or a passing position.
complexpieces: If they resist the sweep by posting, transition to berimbolo for a back take
Chain with collar drag: if the sweep is blocked, use the collar grip to drag them forward and take the back
Transition to X-guard if they step their far leg close enough
simplifications: Practice the DLR hook insertion and tension maintenance as a standalone drill
Drill the push-pull mechanics — hook pushes one direction, grips pull the other
Start with opponent standing still to learn the sweeping angle before adding resistance

techniques.name: Berimbolo
techniques.goal: Invert underneath the opponent from DLR guard to take their back or complete a sweep by rotating under their base.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, preparing to invert. Body will rotate underneath opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Belt grip or pants grip on the near side. Other hand grips their far sleeve or collar for control during rotation.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook on lead leg. Free leg assists the inversion by posting on the mat or on their hip.
initialPosition.hips: Inverting — rolling onto your shoulders and spinning underneath opponent's base.

greenLights: DLR hook is deep and you have a strong belt or pants grip
Opponent's weight is forward over their lead leg — they're loaded for the inversion
You have enough space to invert underneath without being smashed
Opponent is reacting to a sweep attempt — the berimbolo catches them in transition
redLights: Opponent is sprawling back with hips far away — can't get underneath
Your DLR hook has been stripped — no anchor for the rotation
Opponent is driving heavy pressure directly down on you
You lack the comfort or drilling with inversion mechanics
steps: From DLR guard, secure a belt or pants grip. Pull opponent's weight forward over their lead leg.
Invert underneath them — roll onto your shoulders, spin your hips UNDER their base while maintaining the DLR hook and belt grip.
As you rotate underneath, look for their back. Your DLR leg transitions to become a hook. Use your free hand to secure a seatbelt or second hook.
Complete the back take by inserting both hooks and securing seatbelt control. Or, if they sprawl, complete the sweep to top position.
complexpieces: Chain with kiss of the dragon if the berimbolo angle is blocked
If they sprawl during the berimbolo, come up with a single leg takedown
Fake the berimbolo and redirect to a classic DLR sweep when they overreact
simplifications: Practice inversion rolls solo — get comfortable being upside down before adding a partner
Drill the berimbolo motion with a cooperative partner at slow speed
Break into phases: (1) DLR to inversion, (2) rotation under, (3) back take or sweep finish

techniques.name: Babybolo
techniques.goal: A simplified berimbolo-style back take using DLR control to off-balance and invert underneath the opponent without requiring full inversion depth.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard. Preparing for a shallow inversion — less depth than full berimbolo.
initialPosition.grips: Belt or pants grip on the near side. Other hand controls sleeve or collar for stability.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook on lead leg. Free leg posts on the mat or their hip to assist the rotation.
initialPosition.hips: Rotating underneath but with less depth — staying closer to the surface.

greenLights: DLR hook is established and opponent's weight is forward
You want to take the back but a full berimbolo feels too deep
Opponent is reacting to sweeps and their base is momentarily disrupted
You have a strong belt grip to guide the rotation
redLights: Opponent has heavy sprawl pressure preventing any inversion
DLR hook has been cleared — no anchor point
Opponent is actively backstepping to counter inversion attempts
You don't have a belt or pants grip for the rotation control
steps: From DLR guard, secure belt or pants grip and pull opponent's weight forward.
Begin a shallow inversion — rotate your hips underneath but don't go as deep as a full berimbolo. Keep the movement compact.
Use the DLR hook and belt grip to expose their back as you rotate. Your free leg comes around to become a hook.
Insert hooks and secure seatbelt. Complete the back take from a closer, more controlled distance than a full berimbolo.
complexpieces: If the babybolo stalls, commit deeper for a full berimbolo
Chain with collar drag — if they resist the inversion, drag them forward instead
Transition to single leg X if you can't complete the back take
simplifications: Practice the shallow inversion motion solo — it's less intimidating than full berimbolo
Drill with a cooperative partner focusing on the back exposure, not the full take
Master the DLR hook and belt grip connection first — the rotation builds on that

techniques.name: Kiss of the Dragon
techniques.goal: Invert under the opponent's leg and rotate around to take the back by threading through underneath their base from DLR guard.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard. Preparing to thread under opponent's leg and come out behind them.
initialPosition.grips: Pants grip on the near leg. Other hand may grip far ankle, sleeve, or mat for rotation control.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook on lead leg. Free leg assists the threading motion under their body.
initialPosition.hips: Inverting and threading under their lead leg, rotating to emerge behind them.

greenLights: DLR hook is deep and you have pants control on the near leg
Opponent's weight is forward — space exists to thread underneath
Opponent is not sprawling — their hips are accessible
You're comfortable with inversion and can see the path to their back
redLights: Opponent is sprawling with hips very far back — no space to thread
They've stripped your DLR hook and you have no anchor
Opponent is aware and blocking the inversion with crossface pressure
You're not comfortable inverting — forced attempts risk neck injury
steps: From DLR guard, control the near-side pants and begin inverting. Thread your body UNDER their lead leg, rotating on your shoulders.
As you thread through, your DLR hook guides you around their leg. Spin underneath their base toward their back side.
Emerge behind them with your head on the far side. Immediately secure a seatbelt grip (one arm over, one under).
Insert hooks and establish full back control. If they turn to face you, complete a sweep to top position instead.
complexpieces: Chain with berimbolo — if kiss of the dragon path is blocked, redirect to berimbolo angle
If they sprawl as you thread, transition to a crab ride for back control
Use as a counter when opponent tries to pass with a knee slice — thread under the slicing leg
simplifications: Practice the threading/inversion path solo with no partner first
Drill with a cooperative partner standing still to learn the rotation angle
Focus on the shoulder roll and neck safety — never force the inversion

techniques.name: Sit-Up Sweep (DLR Variation)
techniques.goal: Come up to a seated position while maintaining the DLR hook and drive forward for a sweep, using the hook as an anchor while wrestling up.

initialPosition.body: Transitioning from lying in DLR guard to sitting up. Driving forward into opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Collar grip or sleeve grip pulling opponent toward you. Other hand posts on the mat to help you sit up.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook maintained on lead leg. Free leg posts on the mat for base as you rise.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and upward as you sit up into opponent's base.

greenLights: Opponent's weight is back — they're leaning away and not pressuring forward
You have a strong collar or sleeve grip to pull yourself up
The DLR hook gives you an anchor to drive forward from
Opponent is not expecting you to sit up — they're focused on standing guard play
redLights: Opponent is pressuring forward heavily — sitting up into their pressure is risky
You've lost the DLR hook — no anchor to drive from
Your collar/sleeve grip is too weak to pull yourself upright
Opponent is actively trying to pass and you need to defend first
steps: From DLR guard, secure a strong collar or sleeve grip. Post your free hand on the mat behind you.
Sit up explosively while maintaining the DLR hook. Pull yourself toward them using the grip.
As you come up to seated, drive FORWARD into opponent. The DLR hook acts as an anchor preventing them from retreating.
Complete the sweep by driving them backward or transition to a single leg takedown from the seated position.
complexpieces: If they resist the sit-up by pushing you back, immediately attack a collar drag to their back
Transition to a single leg: release the DLR hook and attack the same leg
Chain with classic DLR sweep: sit up (they push back), immediately lie back and sweep the opposite direction
simplifications: Practice the sit-up motion from DLR as a solo movement drill
Drill maintaining the DLR hook while sitting up — the hook often slips during the transition
Start with partner offering light resistance to learn the forward drive angle

techniques.name: Balloon Sweep (DLR)
techniques.goal: Use grips and leg extension to elevate the opponent overhead from DLR guard, launching them over you for a sweep.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard. Scooting underneath opponent to load their weight onto your feet.
initialPosition.grips: Collar and sleeve grips pulling opponent forward. Both grips pull their weight over you.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook releases and both feet transition to opponent's hips/stomach for elevation. Legs coiled, ready to extend.
initialPosition.hips: Underneath opponent's center of gravity, positioned to launch them overhead.

greenLights: Opponent's weight is fully committed forward over you
You can load their weight onto your feet for the elevation
Strong collar and sleeve grips prevent them from posting their hands
Opponent is leaning forward trying to pass or disengage
redLights: Opponent is sitting back with hips away — can't load their weight
You've lost a grip and they can post a hand to block
Opponent has a very wide low base
Your hips aren't underneath them enough for proper elevation angle
steps: From DLR guard, pull opponent forward with collar and sleeve grips. Scoot your hips underneath them.
Release the DLR hook and place both feet on their hips or stomach. Load their weight onto your feet.
EXTEND legs explosively overhead while PULLING the grips past your head. Launch them over you.
Roll through to top position as they land. Maintain at least one grip for immediate control.
complexpieces: If they base out during the elevation, redirect to a lateral DLR sweep
Chain with overhead sweep: same concept but different leg angle and grip adjustment
Use single spider (one foot on bicep) variation for more controlled elevation
simplifications: Practice loading their weight onto your feet — the most important part of the setup
Drill the hip scoot underneath as a separate movement
Start with a cooperative/lighter partner to learn elevation timing

techniques.name: DLR to X-Guard Sweep
techniques.goal: Transition from DLR guard to X-guard by inserting both hooks around opponent's leg, then sweep from the X-guard leg entanglement.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, transitioning underneath opponent to establish X-guard hooks.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve or ankle grip controlling opponent's upper body or far leg. Hands adjust as you transition to X-guard.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook transitions: one leg hooks behind opponent's near knee (bottom hook), other leg places foot on their far hip/thigh (top hook) — forming the X.
initialPosition.hips: Scooting underneath opponent's center of gravity for the X-guard position.

greenLights: Opponent steps their far leg close enough to establish the second X-guard hook
Your DLR hook is deep and you can transition it to the bottom X-guard hook
Opponent is standing with weight forward — ideal for X-guard entry
You can scoot your hips underneath their base
redLights: Opponent's legs are too far apart to establish both X-guard hooks
Opponent is stepping back away from you — can't get underneath
Your DLR hook has been stripped during the transition
Opponent is kneeling — X-guard needs them standing or in combat base
steps: From DLR guard, scoot your hips under opponent's center of gravity. Control their sleeve or far ankle.
Transition your DLR hook to become the bottom X-guard hook behind their near knee. Place your other foot on their far hip as the top hook.
Pinch your knees to control the trapped leg. You're now in X-guard with both hooks forming an X around their leg.
Sweep by elevating with the bottom hook while the top hook steers. Push-pull to topple their base. Come up to top position.
complexpieces: If X-guard sweep is blocked, transition to single leg X for ankle lock or different sweep angle
Chain with technical stand-up: come up to your feet from X-guard while controlling their leg
Use the X-guard to take the back by off-balancing forward and circling behind
simplifications: Practice the DLR to X-guard hook transition as a standalone drill
Drill X-guard sweeps separately once the position is established
Focus on the hip scoot underneath — getting deep enough is the key to X-guard

techniques.name: DLR to Single Leg X Sweep
techniques.goal: Drop underneath the opponent from DLR guard to enter single leg X (ashi garami) and sweep by controlling their ankle and pushing their hip.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, transitioning to sit directly underneath opponent with their leg isolated.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's near ankle or pant cuff. Other hand grips sleeve or posts on the mat.
initialPosition.legs: Inside leg hooks behind opponent's near knee (primary hook). Outside foot pushes on their far hip for control and distance.
initialPosition.hips: Directly underneath opponent's base with their leg isolated between yours.

greenLights: Opponent is standing with their lead leg accessible for isolation
You can drop underneath and control their ankle
The DLR hook transitions naturally to the inside SLX hook
Opponent's far leg is within reach of your pushing foot
redLights: Opponent is kneeling — SLX works best against a standing opponent
You can't control their ankle — they'll retract the leg
Opponent is sprawling back, preventing you from getting underneath
Your DLR hook has been stripped during the transition
steps: From DLR guard, scoot underneath opponent. Control their near ankle with your hand.
Transition your DLR hook to the inside SLX hook — behind their near knee. Place your other foot on their far hip.
PUSH their far hip with your foot while PULLING their ankle toward you. This off-balances them backward.
They topple backward or to the side. Come up to top position. Or transition to leg attacks from the SLX position.
complexpieces: From SLX, attack straight ankle lock or heel hook instead of sweeping
If sweep is blocked, insert second hook for full X-guard
Chain with technical stand-up: come up while controlling the ankle for a single leg finish
simplifications: Practice the DLR to SLX transition as an isolated movement drill
Drill the push-pull sweep mechanic from SLX (foot on hip, hand on ankle)
Focus on ankle control — losing the ankle means losing the sweep

techniques.name: Collar Drag to Back Take
techniques.goal: Use a collar drag from DLR guard to spin behind the opponent and secure back control by pulling them forward and past you.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, preparing to pull opponent forward and spin behind them.
initialPosition.grips: Deep cross collar grip — 4 fingers inside, pulling them forward and down. Other hand may grip sleeve or post on the mat.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook on lead leg providing an anchor. Free leg posts on mat or pushes far hip.
initialPosition.hips: Ready to hip escape and spin behind opponent as they're pulled forward.

greenLights: You have a deep cross collar grip and can pull them forward powerfully
Opponent's weight is forward — the drag accelerates their momentum
Your DLR hook anchors you while you spin behind
Opponent isn't defending their back — focused on the guard
redLights: Collar grip is too shallow — insufficient pulling power
Opponent is leaning back with hips away — the drag won't pull them forward
You've lost the DLR hook — no anchor to spin from
Opponent is actively clearing your grips
steps: From DLR guard, feed your hand DEEP into their cross collar (4 fingers inside). Establish a strong pulling grip.
DRAG them forward and down with the collar grip. Pull them past your body — their momentum takes them over you.
As they stumble forward, release the DLR hook, hip escape, and spin BEHIND them. You're moving to their back as they fall forward.
Secure seatbelt grip (one arm over shoulder, one under armpit) and insert hooks. Establish full back control.
complexpieces: If the drag doesn't fully expose the back, transition to a single leg from the seated position
Chain: collar drag (they resist) → immediately hit a classic DLR sweep in the opposite direction
Use the arm drag variation if the collar drag is blocked — switch to two-on-one on their arm
simplifications: Practice the collar drag pull as an isolated grip and pull drill
Drill the spin-behind movement separately from the collar drag
Start with partner standing still to learn the dragging angle and back take path

techniques.name: DLR to Matrix
techniques.goal: From DLR guard, invert under your opponent while maintaining hook and grip control, rotating beneath them to expose their back and enter the Matrix position for a back take or sweeping finish.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, preparing for inversion to the Matrix position. Body rotates under opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Belt or pants grip on near side. Other hand controls sleeve, collar, or far ankle for rotation stability.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook deep on lead leg. Free leg assists the inversion and rotation under opponent.
initialPosition.hips: Inverting and rotating — threading underneath opponent similar to berimbolo but entering the Matrix angle.

greenLights: DLR hook is deep and you have a strong belt/pants grip
Opponent's weight is forward — space to invert underneath
You're comfortable with inversion mechanics
Classic DLR sweep or berimbolo has been blocked — Matrix is the redirect
redLights: Opponent is sprawling with heavy downward pressure
DLR hook has been stripped — no anchor for rotation
You're not comfortable inverting — don't force it
Opponent is actively backstepping to prevent inversion entries
steps: From DLR guard, secure belt/pants grip and pull opponent's weight forward. Begin inverting underneath them.
Rotate beneath their base while maintaining the DLR hook — thread through to the Matrix angle (you're underneath but angled to expose their back from a different position than berimbolo).
From the Matrix position, use your hooks and grips to expose their back. Work to secure seatbelt control.
Complete the back take by inserting hooks and controlling the seatbelt. Or finish with a sweep to top position if the back take is blocked.
complexpieces: Chain Matrix with berimbolo — if one angle is blocked, redirect to the other
Use Matrix entry to set up crab ride for back control
If opponent defends the back take, come out with a sweep to top and immediately pass
simplifications: Practice inversion to the Matrix angle as a solo drill first
Drill the DLR to Matrix transition with a cooperative partner at slow speed
Master the berimbolo first — Matrix builds on similar inversion mechanics

techniques.name: DLR Wrestle-Up Sweep
techniques.goal: From DLR guard, off-balance your opponent backward, release the guard, and come up to standing while maintaining control to wrestle up into top position.

initialPosition.body: In DLR guard, transitioning from lying to standing. Wrestling up into opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Collar and sleeve grip, or two-on-one on their arm. Grips pull you up and control them as you stand.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook releases as you stand. Free leg posts on the mat to push yourself up.
initialPosition.hips: Driving upward and forward into opponent as you rise to your feet.

greenLights: Opponent's weight is shifted backward — they're not pressuring you
You have strong pulling grips (collar, sleeve, or two-on-one)
Space exists to come up to your feet
Opponent is expecting guard play, not a wrestling-style stand-up
redLights: Opponent is driving heavy pressure forward on top of you
You can't establish strong enough grips to pull yourself up
Opponent is actively passing and you need to defend the guard first
Your base is compromised and you can't post to stand
steps: From DLR guard, off-balance opponent backward with your hook and grips. Create a moment where they're not pressuring you.
Post your free hand on the mat and begin standing up. Maintain your grips on their collar/sleeve for control.
Release the DLR hook and come to your feet. Drive forward into them with your grips, staying chest-to-chest.
Complete the takedown — single leg, double leg, or body lock — from the standing clinch position. Establish top control.
complexpieces: If they push you back down, re-establish DLR guard immediately and attack sweeps
Chain with arm drag: wrestle up with a two-on-one, then arm drag to the back
Transition to a snap-down if they posture too upright when you stand
simplifications: Practice the stand-up from DLR as a movement drill — hook, off-balance, stand
Drill the wrestling finish separately (single leg, double leg) from standing
Start with partner giving no resistance to learn the transition from guard to standing

techniques.name: Triangle Choke (from DLR)
techniques.goal: Create an angle from DLR guard and trap the opponent's neck and one arm in a triangle choke using your legs.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, creating the arm-in, arm-out positioning needed for the triangle.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve grip on the arm you're pulling across (arm-in). Other hand controls collar or pushes their far arm away (arm-out).
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook releases. One leg shoots HIGH over their shoulder and behind their neck. Other foot pushes on their hip for the angle.
initialPosition.hips: Angled to one side, scooted out for the triangle geometry.

greenLights: Opponent reaches forward with one arm while the other stays back — arm-in, arm-out
You've pulled one sleeve across their centerline with your grip
Opponent's posture is broken forward — close enough to throw your leg over
The DLR angle naturally creates the arm-in, arm-out positioning
redLights: Both of opponent's arms are equally positioned — no arm-in, arm-out yet
Opponent has strong upright posture — too far to reach with your leg
You've lost sleeve control — can't control the arm positioning
Opponent is stepping back and creating distance
steps: From DLR guard, pull one sleeve ACROSS their body (arm-in). Control their posture with your other grip or push the far arm away (arm-out).
Release the DLR hook. Push on their hip with one foot for the angle. Throw your other leg HIGH over their shoulder, behind their neck.
Lock the triangle: back of knee over opposite ankle (figure-four). Angle your body 30° to the side for optimal choke geometry.
Squeeze knees together, pull their head DOWN, lift hips. The trapped arm's shoulder compresses one carotid, your thigh compresses the other.
complexpieces: If they posture inside the triangle, switch to armbar on the trapped arm
If they stack you, transition to omoplata by releasing the lock and controlling the shoulder
Set up from a sweep attempt: they post an arm to resist, creating the arm-in positioning
simplifications: Practice creating the arm-in, arm-out from DLR grips as a drill
Drill the leg throw from DLR angle without locking the triangle
Focus on the angle creation first — the triangle geometry follows the correct angle

techniques.name: Omoplata (from DLR)
techniques.goal: Swing your leg over the opponent's arm from DLR guard to isolate and attack their shoulder with an omoplata shoulder lock.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, creating a perpendicular angle. Rotating body to trap their arm under your leg.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve grip on the arm being attacked, pulling it across. Other hand controls posture or assists rotation.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook releases. Attacking leg swings HIGH over opponent's shoulder. Other foot pushes on their hip for the pivot.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to perpendicular angle relative to opponent's body.

greenLights: Opponent extends an arm — posting, reaching, or pushing
You've created a perpendicular angle from the DLR position
Their posture is broken and the arm is accessible for the leg to swing over
Failed triangle or sweep attempt leaves their arm exposed
redLights: Opponent keeps both arms tight and retracted
Opponent has strong upright posture — too far for the leg swing
You can't create the perpendicular angle from DLR
Opponent is already rolling forward before you can control their hip
steps: From DLR guard, control one sleeve and pull the arm across. Push on their hip with your free foot for the pivot.
Release DLR hook and swing your attacking leg HIGH over their shoulder, draping it across their back. Arm is trapped.
Sit up and control their hip or belt to prevent the forward roll escape. Establish the perpendicular angle.
Lean forward, drive their arm behind their back with your leg pressure on the shoulder. Smooth rotational pressure for the submission.
complexpieces: If they roll forward, follow the roll and sweep to top position
Chain from triangle: if triangle attempt fails, redirect the leg for omoplata
Set up a wrist lock on the trapped arm while maintaining the omoplata
simplifications: Practice the leg swing from DLR over partner's shoulder as a movement drill
Drill sitting up and controlling the hip — the most critical part
Break into phases: (1) pull arm across, (2) pivot and swing leg, (3) sit up and finish

techniques.name: Armbar (from DLR)
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's arm when they post or lose posture from DLR guard, pivoting to a perpendicular armbar position.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard, pivoting to the perpendicular armbar angle. Hips scooting to the side.
initialPosition.grips: Wrist or sleeve grip on the arm being attacked. Other hand controls posture (collar, head) or assists the pivot.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook releases. One foot pushes on their hip for the pivot. Other leg swings over their face for the armbar clamp.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to perpendicular angle, scooted to the side for armbar geometry.

greenLights: Opponent posts a hand on the mat or extends an arm to resist a sweep — arm is exposed
You've controlled their wrist and can pivot your hips for the armbar
Opponent's posture is broken forward and they're leaning into you
One arm is isolated from the DLR grip fighting
redLights: Both arms are retracted tight — nothing to isolate
Opponent has strong upright posture and is far away
You've lost wrist control — they'll retract during the pivot
Opponent is stacking you with heavy forward pressure
steps: From DLR guard, isolate one arm with a tight wrist/sleeve grip. Push on their hip with one foot for the pivot.
Release DLR hook and swing your other leg HIGH over their head/face, clamping down across their neck. Pivot hips to perpendicular.
Clamp BOTH legs tight — one over their face, one across their torso. Thumb points UP (arm extended, elbow facing your hips).
Squeeze knees together, pinch tight, and lift hips UP against their elbow for the submission.
complexpieces: If they stack you, transition to triangle or omoplata — leg is already over their shoulder
Chain with triangle: if they pull arm out, lock up the triangle
Set up from a sweep attempt: when they post to resist, catch the posted arm
simplifications: Practice the hip pivot from DLR to the perpendicular armbar angle
Drill the leg-over-face clamp without finishing the submission
Break into two parts: (1) angle creation and leg placement, (2) finishing hip lift

techniques.name: Loop Choke (from DLR)
techniques.goal: Use collar grips from DLR guard to trap the opponent's neck in a loop choke when they pressure forward into the guard.

initialPosition.body: Lying on your back in DLR guard. Opponent is driving forward with low head position.
initialPosition.grips: Deep cross collar grip — 4 fingers inside, fed across the back of their neck to create the loop. Other hand controls their posture.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook maintains control of their lead leg. Free leg assists with posture control.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the collar grip side, pulling opponent's head into the loop.

greenLights: You've fed a deep collar grip across the back of their neck
Opponent is driving forward with their head low — walking into the choke
DLR hook keeps them anchored and unable to back away
Opponent is not defending their collar
redLights: Opponent has strong upright posture — can't feed the collar deep
Collar grip is too shallow for the loop to be effective
Opponent is pulling away and creating distance
Opponent is actively grip-fighting your collar hand
steps: From DLR guard, feed your hand DEEP into their cross collar (4 fingers inside). Pull the collar across and behind their neck to form the loop.
As opponent pressures forward, tighten the loop by pulling the collar grip toward your chest. Use DLR hook to keep them from backing out.
Control their posture with your free hand — grip behind their head or their sleeve. Pull them DOWN into the collar loop.
Squeeze by pulling the collar tight while your DLR hook prevents escape. The loop compresses the neck arteries.
complexpieces: Set up during their pass attempt — feed the collar as they try to clear your DLR hook
Chain with cross collar choke: if the loop doesn't finish, the grip is set for the second hand
Use the loop choke threat to set up sweeps — they posture to defend, opening sweep angles
simplifications: Practice feeding the collar grip deep behind the neck — depth determines effectiveness
Drill the loop formation against a static partner
Focus on using the DLR hook to prevent them from backing away from the choke

techniques.name: Straight Ankle Lock (from DLR)
techniques.goal: Transition from DLR guard into a leg entanglement and attack the opponent's ankle with a straight ankle lock.

initialPosition.body: Transitioning from DLR guard to a leg entanglement position (SLX or ashi garami). Falling to the side with their leg controlled.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands wrap around opponent's ankle/Achilles area. Wrist blade positioned on the Achilles tendon.
initialPosition.legs: Inside leg hooks behind opponent's knee. Outside leg crosses over their hip or thigh to control the leg.
initialPosition.hips: Extending against the controlled ankle, hips driving into the Achilles tendon for the submission.

greenLights: You've transitioned to SLX or ashi garami from DLR — leg is isolated
You can wrap your hands around their Achilles tendon area
Opponent's foot is controlled and they can't rotate free
The leg entanglement is secure with proper hook placement
redLights: You haven't established a proper leg entanglement yet — position before submission
Opponent has already cleared the knee line and can escape
Your grip on the ankle is too high — on the shin instead of the Achilles
Opponent is actively rolling to escape the entanglement
steps: From DLR guard, transition to SLX or ashi garami by scooting underneath and isolating their leg between yours.
Wrap both hands around their ankle — wrist blade positioned on the Achilles tendon. Grip tight (palm-to-palm or figure-four).
Pinch your knees together to control their leg and prevent rotation. Keep their foot trapped against your chest.
Extend your hips FORWARD against the Achilles while arching back slightly. The hip bridge pressure creates the ankle lock submission.
complexpieces: If they defend by bending the knee, transition to a toe hold
Chain with heel hook (No-Gi): if they clear the straight ankle lock, attack the heel
Use the ankle lock threat to sweep — they defend the sub, you come up on top
simplifications: Practice the DLR to leg entanglement transition as a separate drill
Drill the ankle grip placement on a partner — finding the Achilles is key
Focus on the hip extension finishing mechanic from a static position

techniques.name: Estima Lock
techniques.goal: An advanced foot lock attack from specific DLR entries, attacking the opponent's foot by applying rotational pressure against the ankle and foot joints.

initialPosition.body: Transitioning from DLR guard to an inverted or angled position where opponent's foot is accessible.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands control opponent's foot — one grips the heel, other controls the toes or top of the foot. Wrist bone applies pressure.
initialPosition.legs: Legs wrap or control their leg to prevent escape. DLR hook may still be partially engaged.
initialPosition.hips: Angled to apply rotational pressure against the foot and ankle joint.

greenLights: Opponent's foot is accessible from the DLR transition
You can control the foot with both hands for the lock
Opponent is not wearing shoes (No-Gi) or their foot is exposed
The foot lock angle is achievable from your current position
redLights: You can't control the foot — opponent is pulling it away
Not allowed in your competition ruleset — check IBJJF belt-level rules
You're not in a secure enough position to hold the lock
Opponent has retracted the foot and you're overextended
steps: From DLR guard transition, identify the opportunity to attack the exposed foot during guard play or pass attempts.
Secure the foot with both hands — grip the heel and control the top of the foot. Position your wrist bone for the lock.
Apply rotational pressure against the foot and ankle joint. Your body angle and grip create the submission torque.
Maintain control of their leg with your legs to prevent them from pulling free. Apply smooth, steady pressure.
complexpieces: Chain from a failed sweep: when they base out, their foot becomes accessible for the Estima lock
Combine with straight ankle lock: threaten one, switch to the other
Use as a surprise transition during guard passing exchanges
simplifications: Learn the Estima lock grip mechanics on a cooperative partner first
Practice identifying the foot lock opportunity during DLR transitions
Master straight ankle locks first — Estima lock builds on similar foot control principles

techniques.name: Kimura (from DLR)
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's shoulder with a kimura figure-four grip when they post an arm while defending DLR sweeps.

initialPosition.body: In DLR guard or transitioning to seated. Opponent has posted a hand on the mat to defend a sweep — arm is exposed.
initialPosition.grips: Kimura figure-four grip: one hand grabs opponent's wrist, other reaches OVER their arm and grabs your own wrist.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook may release as you sit up for the kimura. Free leg controls their hip or posts on the mat.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the kimura side, sitting up to secure and finish the grip.

greenLights: Opponent posts a hand on the mat to defend your sweep — arm is isolated
You can reach their wrist and secure the figure-four kimura grip
Opponent is focused on base recovery and not defending the arm
You can sit up enough to apply the kimura leverage
redLights: Opponent's arms are tight to their body — nothing to isolate
Opponent immediately grabs their belt or pants to defend the grip
You can't sit up enough to apply proper kimura leverage
Opponent is pulling away and you can't maintain wrist control
steps: From DLR guard, attempt a sweep. When opponent posts their hand to resist, grab their wrist with your same-side hand.
Sit up and reach your other hand OVER their arm, grabbing your own wrist — figure-four kimura grip secured.
Pull their elbow tight to your chest. Control the arm close to your body.
Rotate their wrist behind their back. Apply smooth rotational pressure on the shoulder joint. If needed, release DLR hook and step over their head for better leverage.
complexpieces: If they resist the kimura, use the grip to sweep them
Chain: sweep attempt → they post → kimura on posted arm → they defend → sweep the other direction
Transition to guillotine if they duck their head while defending the kimura
simplifications: Practice recognizing the posted arm opportunity during DLR sweep drills
Drill the figure-four grip from a seated DLR position
Focus on the sit-up transition from lying DLR to sitting with the kimura grip

techniques.name: Back Take to Chokes (from DLR)
techniques.goal: Use berimbolo, kiss of the dragon, or other DLR transitions to take the opponent's back and finish with chokes (RNC, bow and arrow, collar chokes).

initialPosition.body: Behind opponent after a successful back take from DLR guard. Chest on their back, seatbelt control.
initialPosition.grips: Seatbelt grip: one arm over opponent's shoulder, one under their armpit. Transitioning to collar grips (Gi) or RNC grip.
initialPosition.legs: Both hooks inserted inside opponent's thighs. Or body triangle locked around their waist.
initialPosition.hips: Tight against their back, slightly offset to the choking arm side.

greenLights: You've successfully taken the back from a DLR transition (berimbolo, collar drag, etc.)
Seatbelt is secure with both hooks in
Opponent's chin is accessible for the choking arm
Opponent is focused on escaping the back position, not defending the choke
redLights: Back control isn't established — hooks are not in yet
Opponent has already turned to face you — back position is lost
Your seatbelt grip has been broken and they're escaping
You have no collar grip (Gi) and can't get under the chin (No-Gi)
steps: Establish back control with seatbelt grip and hooks after a successful DLR back take transition.
For RNC: fight the choking arm under their chin. Grab your own bicep (figure-four). Hand behind their head. Squeeze.
For Bow and Arrow (Gi): feed deep into far-side collar. Grab far pant leg. Fall to the side and stretch them like drawing a bow.
For collar chokes (Gi): establish cross collar grips and squeeze elbows together for the choke.
complexpieces: If they defend the RNC, switch to bow and arrow. If bow and arrow is blocked, switch to armbar from the back
Chain submissions: RNC attempt → they defend → collar choke → they defend → armbar
Use the threat of chokes to force reactions that expose armbar opportunities
simplifications: Focus first on maintaining back control for extended periods before attacking chokes
Practice one choke at a time from the back — master RNC before adding bow and arrow
Drill hand-fighting to get under the chin against a defending partner

techniques.name: Caio Terra Leg Lock
techniques.goal: From DLR or leg entanglement positions, isolate the opponent's leg and attack the knee or ankle with a controlled leg lock while maintaining hook and base.

initialPosition.body: Transitioning from DLR guard into a leg entanglement. Body angled to isolate opponent's leg for the attack.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's foot or ankle. Hands wrap to secure the leg lock grip specific to the Caio Terra variation.
initialPosition.legs: Legs control opponent's trapped leg — inside hook behind knee, outside leg crosses over for control.
initialPosition.hips: Positioned to apply controlled extension or rotation against the knee or ankle joint.

greenLights: Opponent's leg is isolated in a proper entanglement from DLR
You have a secure grip on the ankle or foot for the leg lock
The knee line is controlled — opponent can't simply pull the leg free
The rule set allows leg locks at your competition level
redLights: Leg entanglement isn't properly established — position before submission
Opponent has cleared the knee line and can escape
Not permitted at your competition belt level (check IBJJF rules)
Your grip on the leg is insecure and opponent is actively rotating to escape
steps: From DLR guard, transition into a leg entanglement by isolating opponent's lead leg between yours.
Secure the Caio Terra leg lock grip — control the foot/ankle with both hands in the specific grip configuration.
Maintain knee line control with your legs — pinch knees and control their trapped leg to prevent escape or rotation.
Apply the leg lock with controlled pressure — extension against the knee or rotation against the ankle. Smooth, steady pressure.
complexpieces: Chain with straight ankle lock: if Caio Terra grip is defended, switch to standard ankle lock
If they rotate to escape, follow the rotation and transition to a different leg lock angle
Use the leg lock threat to sweep: when they defend the submission, come up on top
simplifications: Master the basic DLR to leg entanglement transition first
Practice the specific Caio Terra grip mechanics on a cooperative partner
Drill knee line control as a separate positional exercise

techniques.name: Armbar Shotgun
techniques.goal: From a high-angle DLR setup, swing your leg over the opponent's arm and torso in a fast, explosive motion to finish a tight armbar.

initialPosition.body: In DLR guard with a high angle — hips elevated and close to opponent. Preparing for an explosive leg swing.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve or wrist grip on the arm being attacked. Other hand controls collar, head, or free arm.
initialPosition.legs: DLR hook provides the anchor. Free leg is loaded for the explosive swing over opponent's arm and torso.
initialPosition.hips: Elevated and angled high — close to the opponent for the shotgun-style leg swing.

greenLights: Opponent extends an arm — posting, reaching, or defending a sweep
You have the high angle needed for the explosive leg swing
Wrist control is secure on the target arm
Opponent is not expecting the explosive transition — they're defending something else
redLights: Both of opponent's arms are retracted and tight
You don't have the high angle — hips are too low for the shotgun motion
Wrist control is insecure — they'll pull the arm during the swing
Opponent is already sprawling away with hips back
steps: From DLR guard, establish a high angle with your hips elevated. Control the target arm at the wrist or sleeve.
In one EXPLOSIVE motion, swing your free leg over their arm and torso — shotgun style. The speed of the leg swing is what makes this work.
Clamp both legs tight as the swing completes — one over their face/neck, one across their torso. Thumb pointing UP.
Squeeze knees together and lift hips against their elbow. The explosive entry plus tight clamp creates immediate submission pressure.
complexpieces: If they bend the arm to defend, transition to triangle with the leg already over their shoulder
Chain from a sweep attempt: they post to resist, arm is exposed for the shotgun armbar
If the armbar doesn't finish, transition to omoplata with the leg already in position
simplifications: Practice the explosive leg swing over a partner's arm from DLR as a speed drill
Drill from a static high-angle DLR position without finishing
Break into two parts: (1) achieve high-angle DLR with wrist control, (2) explosive swing and finish

techniques.name: Back Step Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Step backward and around the DLR hook to clear it and pass the guard by removing the hook's leverage.

initialPosition.body: Standing in opponent's DLR guard. They have a hook on your lead leg. Posture upright, preparing to backstep.
initialPosition.grips: Hands controlling opponent's legs, pants, or stripping their grips. Free hand may control their free leg or collar.
initialPosition.legs: Lead leg is hooked by DLR. You will step this leg BACKWARD and around to clear the hook.
initialPosition.hips: Turning hips away from the hook as you backstep, removing the hook's leverage angle.

greenLights: You can feel the DLR hook and know which direction to step to clear it
Opponent's grips are manageable — you can backstep without being swept
Your balance is solid and you won't stumble during the backstep
Opponent is committing to the DLR hook and not anticipating the backstep
redLights: Opponent has very strong sleeve AND collar grips — backstepping may expose you to sweeps
Your balance is compromised and a backstep would leave you off-base
Opponent is already inverting for berimbolo — a backstep may accelerate their back take
You don't have control of their free leg — it may entangle you during the backstep
steps: From standing in their DLR guard, control their free leg and strip upper body grips where possible.
Step your hooked leg BACKWARD and around, turning your hips away from the hook. The backstep removes the hook's leverage.
As the hook clears, immediately drive forward to prevent reguarding. Control their legs.
Consolidate the pass — establish side control, knee on belly, or continue to a toreando pass if their legs are still in the way.
complexpieces: Chain with leg drag: as you backstep, drag their hook leg across for an immediate pass
If the backstep clears the hook, immediately drop into knee slice for a fast pass
Use the backstep to enter a leg entanglement (leg lock) instead of passing
simplifications: Practice the backstep motion as a solo footwork drill
Drill the backstep against a static DLR hook to learn the clearing angle
Focus on controlling their free leg BEFORE backstepping — this prevents counter-attacks

techniques.name: Knee Slice Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Clear the DLR hook and drive your knee across the opponent's legs to pass their guard and reach side control.

initialPosition.body: Standing or in combat base in opponent's DLR guard. Working to clear the hook and transition to knee slice.
initialPosition.grips: Near arm: crossface or collar grip. Far hand: controlling their pants, hook leg, or stripping their grips.
initialPosition.legs: Lead knee drops across opponent's thigh after clearing the DLR hook. Rear leg posted back for drive.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and LOW through the center as the knee slices across.

greenLights: You've cleared the DLR hook enough to insert your knee across their thigh
You have a crossface or collar grip to flatten them
Their free leg is controlled and not creating a new guard
Opponent is flat on their back and not re-hooking
redLights: DLR hook is still deep — clear it before attempting to knee slice
Opponent has strong collar grip pulling your posture down
They're inverting as you knee slice — may expose your back
Opponent has transitioned to SLX or X-guard during your approach
steps: Strip the DLR hook by pushing their hook leg away or backstepping to clear it. Immediately close distance.
Drop your near knee across their thigh and secure crossface or collar grip. Drive your shoulder into their jaw.
Slice your knee FORWARD through their legs with constant chest pressure. Keep hips LOW — don't rise up.
Clear past their legs and consolidate side control with crossface and hip control.
complexpieces: If they re-hook during the slice, backstep to clear and retry
Chain with toreando: fake the knee slice, redirect to toreando when they react
Use a collar grip to threaten a choke during the knee slice
simplifications: Practice clearing the DLR hook as a separate drill before adding the knee slice
Drill the knee slice motion from a static position without the hook
Focus on maintaining shoulder pressure throughout — the most common mistake is lifting up

techniques.name: Leg Drag Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Control the opponent's DLR hook leg and drag it across your body to pass with tight hip-to-hip control.

initialPosition.body: Standing in opponent's DLR guard. Grabbing their hook leg to drag it across your body.
initialPosition.grips: One hand grips opponent's hook-leg pant cuff at the ankle/shin. Other hand controls their collar, belt, or far leg.
initialPosition.legs: Stepping to the drag side as you pull their leg across. Strong base maintained.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and to the side, pinning their dragged leg against your hip.

greenLights: You can grab their DLR hook leg and redirect it across your body
Their upper body grips are weak enough that the drag won't trigger a sweep
You have a secondary grip (collar/belt) for control during the pass
Opponent is flat on their back and not inverting
redLights: Opponent has very strong collar grip — dragging may pull you into a sweep
They're actively inverting as you grab the leg — could lead to berimbolo
You can't control the leg — they keep regripping with the DLR hook
Opponent has transitioned to a different guard during the drag attempt
steps: From standing in their DLR guard, grab their hook-leg pant cuff at the ankle or shin.
DRAG their leg across your body toward your opposite hip. Step to the drag side.
PIN their dragged leg against your hip with your body. Drive your hips close to theirs — hip-to-hip.
Drop into side control on the drag side. Their hips are turned away, making reguarding very difficult.
complexpieces: Chain with backstep: if the drag is blocked, backstep to clear the hook instead
Use the leg drag position to set up a back take by stepping over
If they reguard one leg, immediately switch to knee slice
simplifications: Practice the leg drag grip and motion from a static DLR hook
Drill the hip-to-hip pinning pressure from a static position
Focus on the step to the drag side — footwork makes or breaks this pass

techniques.name: Toreando Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Control the opponent's legs from DLR guard and circle around them quickly using the toreando (bullfighter) pass to reach side control.

initialPosition.body: Standing in opponent's DLR guard. Stripping hooks and controlling both legs for the toreando.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands gripping opponent's pant legs at the knees or shins. Redirecting both legs to one side.
initialPosition.legs: Standing with explosive lateral movement ready. Strong base for the direction change.
initialPosition.hips: Mobile and ready to explode laterally like a bullfighter.

greenLights: You've cleared the DLR hook and can control both legs
Opponent's upper body grips are broken — they can't pull you
You have both pant grips at the knees/shins
Opponent is flat on their back after losing the DLR hook
redLights: DLR hook is still active — clear it first
Opponent has strong collar or sleeve grips pulling you
They're transitioning to a different guard as you strip the DLR
You can only control one leg — need both for toreando
steps: Clear the DLR hook and strip upper body grips. Grab both pant legs at the knees or shins.
Push one leg DOWN to the mat while directing the other leg across. Choose the passing side.
EXPLODE laterally like a bullfighter — move hips quickly to the side, passing around their legs. Speed before they regrip.
Drop into side control: crossface and hip control. Sprawl legs back and drive chest-to-chest pressure.
complexpieces: Fake toreando one direction, then switch sides when they react
Chain with leg drag: if the toreando is blocked, drag the near leg across
If they re-establish DLR during the pass, immediately backstep to clear
simplifications: Practice the toreando lateral explosion as a solo agility drill
Drill clearing the DLR hook + toreando as a two-step combination
Start with the hook already cleared to focus on the toreando passing angle

techniques.name: Stack Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Drive the opponent's knees toward their chest from DLR guard to collapse their guard structure and pass using stacking pressure.

initialPosition.body: Standing or kneeling in opponent's DLR guard. Driving forward to collapse their leg structure.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's legs at the knees or thighs, pushing them toward their chest.
initialPosition.legs: Stepping forward with one foot up for drive. Other knee on mat or both feet up.
initialPosition.hips: Driving FORWARD and DOWN, stacking opponent's hips over their shoulders.

greenLights: DLR hook is neutralized or weakened — you can drive forward
You can push their legs toward their chest effectively
Opponent is relatively flat and can't maintain distance
You have control of both legs for the stacking pressure
redLights: DLR hook is deep and pulling you off-balance — clear it first
Opponent has strong grips preventing you from closing distance
They're inverting as you stack — could expose your back
Opponent is very flexible and stacking has minimal effect
steps: Clear the DLR hook or neutralize its effectiveness. Drive forward, pushing opponent's knees toward their chest.
Stack their hips OVER their head with shoulder pressure into their thighs. Keep head on the passing side.
Walk around to the passing side, maintaining constant stacking pressure. Don't let them unstack.
Clear the legs and drop into side control. Secure crossface and hip control.
complexpieces: Chain with toreando: if they kick out of the stack, redirect to toreando
If their grips prevent stacking, strip grips first then immediately re-drive
Use the stack pressure to set up a submission during the pass (collar choke, d'arce)
simplifications: Practice the forward stacking drive as a pressure drill
Drill clearing the DLR hook then immediately stacking as a combination
Focus on keeping constant forward pressure — the most common mistake is letting them re-establish distance

techniques.name: Long Step Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Step the hooked leg far back to break the DLR hook and circle around the guard, using a long backstep to create distance from the hook.

initialPosition.body: Standing in opponent's DLR guard. Preparing to take a very long step backward with the hooked leg.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's free leg and stripping upper body grips. Hands stabilize during the long step.
initialPosition.legs: Hooked leg takes a very long step BACK and away, breaking the DLR hook through distance.
initialPosition.hips: Turning away from the hook as the long step clears it, then immediately circling back to pass.

greenLights: The DLR hook can be broken with distance — a long step will pull the hook off
You have enough balance to take a long step without being swept
Opponent's grips are weak enough that the long step won't create a sweep opportunity
You can control their free leg to prevent them from transitioning guards
redLights: Opponent has very strong collar grip that will pull you off-balance during the step
The long step would leave you stretched out and vulnerable
Opponent is inverting — the long step gives them space to berimbolo
Your balance is already compromised
steps: From standing in their DLR guard, control their free leg. Prepare to step your hooked leg far back.
Take a LONG step backward with the hooked leg — step far enough that their hook loses tension and drops off.
As the hook clears, immediately circle your hips back toward the opponent. Don't leave space.
Close the distance and pass — use toreando, knee slice, or drop into side control depending on their reaction.
complexpieces: Chain with backstep: if long step doesn't fully clear the hook, add a backstep rotation
Immediately drive into knee slice as the hook clears for a fast pass
If they transition to reverse DLR during the step, address the new hook before continuing
simplifications: Practice the long step motion as a solo footwork drill
Drill against a static DLR hook to learn how far you need to step
Focus on immediately closing distance after the step — don't pause at long range

techniques.name: Headquarters Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Control the opponent's legs from headquarters position to prevent DLR hooks from being established, using the position as a launching pad for passes.

initialPosition.body: Standing or in combat base with one knee between opponent's legs (headquarters position). Preventing hooks.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling both of opponent's legs — one hand on the near ankle/shin, other on the far knee or hip. Keeping legs separated.
initialPosition.legs: Lead knee wedged between opponent's legs, preventing them from hooking. Rear leg posted for base.
initialPosition.hips: Centered and low, controlling the space between opponent's legs.

greenLights: You've established headquarters position between their legs
DLR hook is prevented by your knee positioning
You have control of both their legs from the headquarters stance
Opponent can't re-establish guard hooks from this position
redLights: Opponent has already established DLR hook — headquarters is a preventive position
You're off-balance in headquarters and vulnerable to sweeps
Opponent has strong upper body grips pulling you out of position
They're transitioning to butterfly guard underneath your headquarters
steps: As you approach or after clearing the DLR hook, establish headquarters: knee between their legs, controlling both ankles/shins.
Keep their legs separated and prevent any hooks from being inserted. Your knee wedge blocks their guard establishment.
From headquarters, choose your pass: knee slice (slice lead knee across), toreando (push legs to one side), or leg drag.
Execute the pass with momentum from the headquarters position. The position gives you a head start on any passing technique.
complexpieces: Chain multiple passes from headquarters: fake knee slice, switch to toreando, then leg drag
If they pummel a hook in, immediately address it before it becomes full DLR
Use headquarters to set up a body lock pass by closing distance and wrapping
simplifications: Practice establishing headquarters from standing as a positional drill
Drill launching passes from headquarters against light resistance
Focus on the knee positioning between their legs — this is the key to the position

techniques.name: Leg Weave Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Thread your arm between the opponent's legs to weave and staple them together, applying shoulder pressure while circling to collapse their DLR guard and pass.

initialPosition.body: Standing or kneeling in opponent's DLR guard. Threading one arm between their legs to weave them together.
initialPosition.grips: One arm threads between opponent's legs and grabs their far pant leg or controls the far thigh. Other hand controls collar or near leg.
initialPosition.legs: Strong base for driving forward. Closing distance as you weave.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward with shoulder pressure, collapsing their guard structure.

greenLights: DLR hook has been cleared or weakened enough to thread your arm through
You can reach between their legs and grab the far pant leg/thigh
Opponent is relatively flat and you can apply shoulder pressure
Their guard structure is collapsing as you weave the legs together
redLights: DLR hook is too deep to thread your arm between their legs
Opponent has strong frames preventing you from closing distance
They're inverting or transitioning to a different guard as you weave
Your arm is trapped between their legs and they're attacking leg locks
steps: Clear or neutralize the DLR hook. Thread your near arm BETWEEN opponent's legs, reaching for their far pant leg or thigh.
Grab the far leg and pull it toward you, stapling both of their legs together. Their guard structure collapses.
Apply shoulder pressure into their thighs/chest as you circle to the passing side. Keep their legs stapled.
Walk around to side control. Release the weave and secure crossface and hip control.
complexpieces: Chain with stack pass: if the weave stalls, use it to stack their hips instead
If they re-establish hooks during the weave, immediately switch to toreando
Use the leg weave position to set up a d'arce or anaconda choke on the way past
simplifications: Practice the arm threading motion between their legs as a drill
Drill the leg weave against a static guard to learn the stapling mechanics
Focus on maintaining shoulder pressure while circling — don't lose pressure

techniques.name: X Pass (vs DLR)
techniques.goal: Control one of the opponent's legs while stepping over with your opposite leg in an X-shaped configuration, clearing DLR hooks and driving across to side control.

initialPosition.body: Standing in opponent's DLR guard. Controlling their near leg and preparing to step across.
initialPosition.grips: One hand controls opponent's near-side pant leg/ankle. Other hand controls their collar, belt, or far leg.
initialPosition.legs: Far leg steps OVER and across their body in an X-pattern as you control the near leg. Lead leg follows.
initialPosition.hips: Driving across their body, using the X-step to clear their guard structure.

greenLights: DLR hook has been cleared or you can step over it
You have a solid grip on their near leg for control
The X-step path is clear — their legs aren't blocking the crossover
Opponent is flat and not actively transitioning guards
redLights: DLR hook is deep and blocks your leg from stepping over
Opponent has strong grips that could sweep you mid-step
Their free leg is framing and preventing the crossover
Balance isn't stable enough for the X-step
steps: From standing, clear the DLR hook. Control their near leg with one hand. Grip their collar or belt with the other.
Step your FAR leg OVER and across their body — the X-step. Drive your hips across as you step.
Apply chest and shoulder pressure as you land on the passing side. Control their near leg to prevent reguarding.
Consolidate side control with crossface and hip control. Drive chest-to-chest pressure.
complexpieces: Chain with toreando: if the X pass is blocked, redirect to toreando the other direction
Use the X pass to set up knee on belly instead of side control for immediate pressure
If they reguard during the pass, immediately backstep and try again
simplifications: Practice the X-step crossover as a solo footwork drill
Drill the X pass against a static guard to learn the stepping angle
Focus on controlling the near leg throughout — losing it means losing the pass

positions.name: Butterfly Guard
positions.description: Seated open guard where the bottom player uses both instep hooks inside the opponent's thighs to elevate, off-balance, and create sweeping and submission opportunities. Emphasizes an active, seated posture with strong upper body grips.

techniques.name: Butterfly Sweep (Classic)
techniques.goal: Elevate the opponent with one hook while controlling the upper body to sweep them over to one side.

initialPosition.body: Seated upright in butterfly guard with both instep hooks inside opponent's thighs. Chest close to opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Underhook on the sweeping side — arm deep under opponent's armpit. Other hand controls their far wrist, sleeve, or collar.
