initialPosition.legs: Both butterfly hooks (insteps) inside opponent's thighs. Sweeping-side hook will elevate. Other hook provides support.
initialPosition.hips: Slightly angled toward the sweeping side, loaded to elevate and roll.

greenLights: You have a deep underhook on the sweeping side
Opponent's weight is centered or slightly on the sweeping side
Your head is tight against their chest/shoulder — connected
The elevating hook is loaded and ready to lift
redLights: Opponent has a strong overhook or whizzer neutralizing your underhook
Opponent is sprawled back with hips far away — hooks can't bite
Your posture is broken and you're being flattened onto your back
Opponent has cross-faced you and driven your head to the mat
steps: Sit up into opponent's chest with a deep underhook on the sweeping side. Control their far arm with your other hand to prevent posting.
Angle your body slightly toward the sweeping side. Load your weight onto the sweeping-side hook.
ELEVATE with the hook — kick your instep up into their thigh while falling to the opposite side. Your underhook pulls them over you.
Complete the sweep by rolling them over. Come up to top position with your head driving into their chest. Establish side control or mount.
complexpieces: If they whizzer the underhook, switch to an arm drag on the other side and sweep that direction
Chain with guillotine: fake the sweep to draw their head down, then attack the neck
If they post a hand to block, transition to X-guard underneath for a different sweep angle
simplifications: Practice the elevating hook motion solo — kick the instep up and fall to the side
Drill the underhook entry from butterfly guard as a separate exercise
Start with partner offering light resistance — focus on the angle and timing of the elevation

techniques.name: Sit-Up Sweep
techniques.goal: Drive forward while elevating with the butterfly hook and come up on top by wrestling up into the opponent.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard, driving forward into opponent. Chest-to-chest contact, sitting up aggressively.
initialPosition.grips: Collar and sleeve grip, or double underhooks, pulling opponent into you. Grips control their upper body as you drive forward.
initialPosition.legs: Both butterfly hooks inside opponent's thighs. Elevating hook lifts as you drive forward.
initialPosition.hips: Driving upward and forward — moving from seated to on top of them.

greenLights: Opponent's weight is shifting backward — they're leaning away from you
You have strong pulling grips (collar/sleeve or double underhooks)
Your hooks are engaged and can elevate as you drive
Opponent is not pressuring forward into you — space to come up
redLights: Opponent is driving heavy pressure forward — you'll get flattened
Your grips are broken and you can't pull yourself into them
Hooks are not engaged — feet are too low or outside their thighs
Opponent has crossface pressure pinning your head down
steps: From butterfly guard, establish strong upper body grips — collar and sleeve or double underhooks.
Drive FORWARD into opponent's chest while simultaneously elevating with one butterfly hook.
Come up from seated to kneeling — your hooks lift them as your body weight drives forward.
Complete the sweep to top position. Establish side control, mount, or transition to a takedown finish.
complexpieces: If they push you back, immediately attack the classic butterfly sweep in the opposite direction
Chain with arm drag: drive forward, they push back, arm drag to the back
Use the sit-up momentum to attack a guillotine if their head drops
simplifications: Practice the sit-up motion from butterfly guard as a solo movement drill
Drill the combination of hook elevation + forward drive with a static partner
Focus on the timing — elevate and drive simultaneously, not sequentially

techniques.name: Arm Drag to Back Take
techniques.goal: Drag the opponent's arm across from butterfly guard and move behind them to secure back control.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard, preparing to pull opponent's arm across and spin behind them.
initialPosition.grips: Two-on-one on opponent's arm — one hand grips their wrist, other grips above their elbow (tricep). Pulling the arm across your body.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks inside their thighs. Hooks assist the angle change as you move to the back.
initialPosition.hips: Pivoting to the side as you drag the arm, creating the angle to get behind them.

greenLights: You've established a two-on-one grip on their arm
Opponent is reaching forward — arm is accessible for the drag
Your butterfly hooks give you enough base to spin behind them
Opponent is not defending the arm drag — focused on passing
redLights: Opponent's arms are tight and retracted — nothing to grab
They're pulling the arm away as you try to drag
Opponent is leaning back with weight away from you — the drag won't pull them forward
You've lost your butterfly hooks and have no base to pivot from
steps: From butterfly guard, grab opponent's wrist with one hand and their tricep with the other — two-on-one grip.
DRAG their arm forcefully across your body. Pull at the wrist while pushing at the tricep to accelerate the drag.
As their arm crosses, pivot your hips and spin BEHIND them. Your butterfly hooks help rotate you around their body.
Secure seatbelt grip (one arm over shoulder, one under armpit) and insert hooks. Complete the back take.
complexpieces: If the back take is blocked, transition to a single leg on the near side
Chain: arm drag (they resist) → immediately classic butterfly sweep (they're now off-balance resisting the drag)
From the back, attack RNC or collar chokes immediately
simplifications: Practice the two-on-one arm drag grip and pull as an isolated drill
Drill the pivot from butterfly to back position without finishing the take
Focus on the drag angle — pull across and DOWN, not just sideways

techniques.name: Wrestle-Up Sweep
techniques.goal: Use butterfly guard to come up to your feet and finish a takedown by wrestling up into a standing clinch or single/double leg.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard, transitioning to standing. Driving upward and into the opponent.
initialPosition.grips: Collar and sleeve, underhook, or two-on-one grip pulling you up and controlling them as you stand.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks release as you stand. Feet post on the mat to push yourself upright.
initialPosition.hips: Driving upward from seated to standing while staying connected to opponent.

greenLights: Opponent's weight is shifted backward — not pressuring you
You have strong grips to pull yourself up and control them
Space exists to come up to your feet without being smashed back down
Opponent is not expecting you to stand — they're defending seated attacks
redLights: Opponent is driving heavy forward pressure — can't stand into it
Your grips are broken and you have nothing to pull up with
Opponent has head control pinning you down
No space to get your feet underneath you
steps: From butterfly guard, off-balance opponent backward with your hooks and grips. Create a moment of reduced pressure.
Post one hand on the mat and begin standing up. Keep your other grip on the opponent for control.
Come to your feet while staying chest-to-chest. Maintain collar/underhook control.
Complete a takedown — single leg, double leg, or body lock — from the standing clinch. Establish top position.
complexpieces: If they push you back down, re-establish butterfly guard and attack sweeps immediately
Chain with arm drag: stand up with a two-on-one, then arm drag to the back
Use the wrestle-up momentum to snap them down if they posture too high
simplifications: Practice the stand-up from butterfly as a solo movement drill
Drill maintaining grips while transitioning from seated to standing
Start with partner giving minimal resistance to learn the balance transition

techniques.name: Butterfly to X-Guard Sweep
techniques.goal: Transition from butterfly guard underneath the opponent to establish X-guard and sweep from the leg entanglement.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard, dropping underneath opponent to establish X-guard hooks around one leg.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's sleeve or ankle. Hands adjust as you transition underneath to X-guard.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks transition: one leg hooks behind opponent's near knee (bottom X hook), other foot pushes on far hip (top X hook).
initialPosition.hips: Scooting underneath opponent's center of gravity for the X-guard position.

greenLights: Opponent stands up or posts on one leg — ideal X-guard entry
You can scoot underneath and establish both X-guard hooks
Opponent's weight is forward over you — loadable for the sweep
Your butterfly hook can transition cleanly to the bottom X hook
redLights: Opponent is kneeling with hips low — X-guard needs them elevated
You can't scoot underneath deep enough for proper hook placement
Opponent is backing away — can't establish the entanglement
Your hooks are being stripped during the transition
steps: From butterfly guard, as opponent stands or elevates, scoot your hips underneath their center of gravity.
Transition one butterfly hook to the bottom X-guard hook behind their near knee. Place your other foot on their far hip as the top hook.
Pinch knees together to control the trapped leg. You're now in X-guard with hooks forming an X around their leg.
Sweep by elevating with the bottom hook while the top hook steers. Push-pull to topple their base. Come up to top position.
complexpieces: If X-guard sweep is blocked, transition to single leg X for ankle lock or different angle
Chain with technical stand-up: come up from X-guard while controlling their leg
Use X-guard to take the back by off-balancing forward and circling behind
simplifications: Practice the butterfly to X-guard hook transition as a standalone drill
Drill X-guard sweeps once the position is established separately
Focus on scooting deep enough underneath — depth determines X-guard effectiveness

techniques.name: Butterfly to Single Leg X Sweep
techniques.goal: Drop underneath from butterfly guard to enter single leg X (ashi garami) and sweep by controlling the ankle and pushing the hip.

initialPosition.body: From butterfly guard, dropping underneath opponent with their leg isolated between yours.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's near ankle with your hand. Other hand grips sleeve or posts for base.
initialPosition.legs: Inside leg hooks behind opponent's near knee (SLX hook). Outside foot pushes on their far hip for distance and control.
initialPosition.hips: Directly underneath opponent's base, their leg isolated between yours.

greenLights: Opponent stands or elevates — one leg accessible for isolation
You can drop underneath and control their ankle from butterfly
Butterfly hook transitions naturally to the inside SLX hook
Opponent's far leg is within reach of your pushing foot
redLights: Opponent is kneeling with hips low — SLX works best against elevated opponents
You can't control their ankle — they'll retract the leg
Opponent is sprawling heavy on top of you
Your butterfly hooks are being cleared before you can transition
steps: From butterfly guard, as opponent stands or posts, scoot underneath them. Control their near ankle with your hand.
Transition your butterfly hook to the inside SLX hook behind their near knee. Place your other foot on their far hip.
PUSH their far hip with your foot while PULLING their ankle toward you. Off-balance them backward.
They topple backward or to the side. Come up to top position. Or transition to leg attacks from SLX.
complexpieces: From SLX, attack straight ankle lock instead of sweeping
If the sweep is blocked, insert second hook for full X-guard
Chain with technical stand-up while controlling the ankle for a single leg finish
simplifications: Practice the butterfly to SLX transition as an isolated drill
Drill the push-pull mechanic from SLX (foot on hip, hand on ankle)
Focus on ankle control first — losing the ankle means losing everything

techniques.name: Guillotine Choke
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's neck with a guillotine choke when they drive forward into your butterfly guard, using head position and arm wrap to choke.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard. Opponent's head is low, driving into your chest. You're wrapping their neck.
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm wraps around their neck — chin strap grip (wrist bone under their throat). Other hand clasps over to lock the grip.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks inside their thighs for elevation and control. Hooks prevent them from pulling away.
initialPosition.hips: Falling slightly to the choking-arm side and elevating hips for choke pressure.

greenLights: Opponent drives their head forward into your chest — neck is exposed
You can wrap your arm around their neck with the wrist bone under their throat
Butterfly hooks prevent them from backing away from the choke
Opponent is focused on passing and not defending their neck
redLights: Opponent keeps their head up and chin tucked — neck not accessible
You can't wrap the neck deep enough for the wrist bone placement
Opponent is pulling away and your hooks can't keep them close
Your grip is on the chin/jaw rather than the throat — won't finish
steps: From butterfly guard, as opponent drives forward with low head, wrap your arm around their neck. Wrist bone directly under their throat.
Clasp your hands together to lock the guillotine grip (palm-to-palm or Marcelotine grip).
Fall slightly to the choking-arm side. Keep butterfly hooks engaged to prevent escape.
SQUEEZE by pulling your elbows toward your chest while lifting your hips UP against their neck. The compression chokes the arteries.
complexpieces: If they defend by turning their head, switch to arm-in guillotine for more pressure
Chain: guillotine attempt (they posture up) → immediately sweep them as their arms defend the choke
Transition to anaconda or d'arce if the guillotine angle shifts
simplifications: Practice the chin strap grip — wrist bone placement under the throat is everything
Drill the wrap from butterfly against a partner driving forward
Focus on the squeeze mechanic: elbows IN, hips UP — not a crank

techniques.name: Arm-in Guillotine
techniques.goal: Trap the opponent's arm inside the guillotine to increase choking pressure and prevent the common defense of pulling the head free.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard with opponent's head low. Their arm is trapped INSIDE the guillotine wrap alongside their neck.
initialPosition.grips: Choking arm wraps around their neck AND their near-side arm. Wrist bone on throat. Other hand clasps to lock the grip.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks inside their thighs. Hooks keep them close and prevent escape. May transition to closed guard or high guard for extra control.
initialPosition.hips: Angling slightly to the choking side. Hips elevate for pressure.

greenLights: Opponent dives their head forward and their arm is inside the wrap
You can secure the arm-in configuration with the wrist bone still on their throat
Butterfly hooks keep them from pulling away
The arm-in position blocks their primary defense (pulling the trapped arm out)
redLights: You can't trap their arm inside — it's too retracted
The arm being inside pushes your wrist off the throat — grip is misaligned
Opponent is pulling their head away and your hooks can't keep them close
Your grip fatigue is setting in and you can't maintain the squeeze
steps: From butterfly guard, as opponent drives forward, wrap your arm around their neck AND their near arm. Trap the arm inside.
Position your wrist bone under their throat. Clasp hands to lock the grip (Marcelotine or palm-to-palm).
Fall to the choking-arm side. Close butterfly guard or throw a leg over their back for extra control.
SQUEEZE elbows toward your chest and ELEVATE hips. The trapped arm's shoulder pushes into the neck, increasing pressure.
complexpieces: If they start to escape, transition to a regular guillotine by releasing the arm
Chain with sweep: if the choke doesn't finish, use the head control to sweep to mount and finish from top
Switch to anaconda choke if the arm-in angle is better suited
simplifications: Practice trapping the arm inside the wrap as a grip drill
Drill the difference between arm-in and standard guillotine positioning
Focus on wrist bone placement — the arm must not push your wrist away from the throat

techniques.name: Kimura (from Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Attack the opponent's shoulder with a kimura figure-four grip when they post an arm while defending sweeps from butterfly guard.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard. Opponent posts a hand on the mat or reaches forward — arm exposed.
initialPosition.grips: Kimura figure-four: one hand grips opponent's wrist, other reaches OVER their arm and grabs your own wrist.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks maintain guard control. May release one hook to step over their head for finishing leverage.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the kimura side, driving into the opponent for control.

greenLights: Opponent posts a hand on the mat to resist a sweep attempt — arm is isolated
You can reach their wrist and secure the figure-four kimura grip
Butterfly hooks keep them close while you attack
Opponent is focused on base recovery, not arm defense
redLights: Opponent's arms are tight to their body — nothing to isolate
They immediately grab their belt or pants to defend the grip
Your hooks are being cleared and you're losing guard position
Opponent is pulling away and you can't maintain wrist control
steps: From butterfly guard, attempt a sweep. When opponent posts their hand to resist, grab their wrist with your same-side hand.
Reach your other hand OVER their arm and grab your own wrist — figure-four kimura grip secured.
Pull their elbow tight to your chest. Control the arm close to your body.
Rotate their wrist behind their back with smooth rotational pressure on the shoulder joint. Step over their head with your leg for extra leverage if needed.
complexpieces: If they resist the kimura, use the grip to sweep them — the kimura grip is a powerful sweeping handle
Chain: sweep attempt → they post → kimura on posted arm → they defend → sweep the other direction
Transition to hip bump sweep using the kimura grip as the driving force
simplifications: Practice recognizing the posted arm during butterfly sweep drills
Drill the figure-four grip quickly from seated butterfly guard
Focus on keeping their elbow tight to your body — space kills the kimura

techniques.name: Triangle Choke (from Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Create an angle from butterfly guard and trap the opponent's neck and one arm in a triangle choke with your legs.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard, creating arm-in, arm-out positioning. Transitioning to lying back for the triangle.
initialPosition.grips: Sleeve grip on the arm being pulled across (arm-in). Other hand controls collar or pushes far arm away (arm-out).
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks release. One leg shoots HIGH over their shoulder behind their neck. Other foot pushes on their hip for the angle.
initialPosition.hips: Scooting out to create the triangle angle, lying back from seated.

greenLights: Opponent reaches with one arm while the other stays back — arm-in, arm-out created
Butterfly guard gives you the seated angle to transition into the triangle
Opponent's posture is broken forward — close enough for the leg to reach
Sleeve control is established to guide the arm across
redLights: Both arms are equally positioned — no arm-in, arm-out yet
Opponent pulls back with strong upright posture — legs can't reach
You've lost sleeve control and can't manage arm positioning
Opponent is disengaging and creating distance
steps: From butterfly guard, pull one sleeve ACROSS their body (arm-in). Push or block the far arm (arm-out).
Release butterfly hooks and lie back slightly. Throw one leg HIGH over their shoulder, behind their neck.
Lock the triangle: back of knee over opposite ankle (figure-four). Angle your body 30° for optimal choke geometry.
Squeeze knees together, pull head DOWN, lift hips. Shoulder compresses one carotid, thigh compresses the other.
complexpieces: If they posture inside the triangle, switch to armbar on the trapped arm
If they stack you, transition to omoplata by releasing the lock
Set up from a sweep: they post to resist, creating the arm-in positioning
simplifications: Practice creating arm-in, arm-out from butterfly grips as a drill
Drill the transition from seated butterfly to lying-back triangle entry
Focus on the 30° angle — the triangle geometry depends on correct angling

techniques.name: Straight Ankle Lock (from Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Enter leg entanglements from butterfly guard and attack the opponent's ankle with a straight ankle lock.

initialPosition.body: Transitioning from butterfly guard to ashi garami (SLX) or leg entanglement. Falling to the side with their leg controlled.
initialPosition.grips: Both hands wrap around opponent's ankle/Achilles. Wrist blade on the Achilles tendon.
initialPosition.legs: Inside leg hooks behind opponent's knee. Outside leg crosses over their hip or thigh for control.
initialPosition.hips: Extending against the controlled ankle, driving into the Achilles for the submission.

greenLights: You've transitioned from butterfly to a leg entanglement — leg is isolated
You can wrap your hands around their Achilles tendon area
Opponent's foot is controlled and they can't rotate free
The entanglement is secure with proper hook placement
redLights: You haven't established proper leg entanglement — position before submission
Opponent has cleared the knee line and can escape
Your grip is too high on the shin instead of the Achilles
Opponent is actively rolling to escape the entanglement
steps: From butterfly guard, transition to SLX or ashi garami by isolating one leg during a sweep attempt or transition.
Wrap both hands around their ankle — wrist blade on the Achilles tendon. Grip tight (palm-to-palm or figure-four).
Pinch knees to control their leg and prevent rotation. Keep foot trapped against your chest.
Extend hips FORWARD against the Achilles while arching back. Hip bridge pressure finishes the ankle lock.
complexpieces: If they defend by bending the knee, transition to toe hold
Chain with heel hook (No-Gi): if they clear the straight ankle lock, attack the heel
Use the ankle lock threat to sweep — they defend the sub, you come up on top
simplifications: Practice the butterfly to leg entanglement transition as a separate drill
Drill the ankle grip placement — finding the Achilles is the key
Focus on the hip extension finishing mechanic from a static ashi garami position

techniques.name: Front Headlock Chokes
techniques.goal: Control the opponent's head from butterfly guard and attack with front headlock-style chokes (guillotine variants, d'arce, anaconda) when they drive forward.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard with opponent's head controlled. Opponent is driving forward with head low — vulnerable to front headlock attacks.
initialPosition.grips: Overhook or snap-down on their head/neck. Transition to specific choke grip (guillotine, d'arce, or anaconda) based on arm position.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks maintain guard control and prevent them from backing away.
initialPosition.hips: Adjusting angle based on the specific choke — may fall to one side or sprawl.

greenLights: Opponent's head is low and driving forward — neck is accessible
You can snap their head down and establish front headlock control
Their arm position determines which choke: arm-in (d'arce/anaconda) or arm-out (guillotine)
Butterfly hooks keep them anchored while you attack
redLights: Opponent keeps their head up with strong posture — can't snap down
They're pulling away and your hooks can't keep them close
You can't establish a choking grip — head control alone isn't enough
Opponent is aware and defending their neck proactively
steps: From butterfly guard, snap opponent's head down or catch it as they drive forward. Establish front headlock control.
Identify arm position: arm-out → guillotine. Arm-in nearside → d'arce. Arm-in farside → anaconda.
Secure the appropriate choke grip. Fall to the choking side or sprawl for angle depending on the specific choke.
SQUEEZE — pull elbows in, elevate hips, compress the neck. Finish the choke with steady pressure.
complexpieces: Chain between guillotine variations: standard → arm-in → d'arce based on their defense
If the choke doesn't finish, use the head control to sweep to top and finish from mount
Snap down to front headlock, then transition to go-behind for back take if choke isn't there
simplifications: Practice the snap-down from butterfly guard as a standalone drill
Drill identifying which choke to use based on arm position
Master one choke (guillotine) before adding d'arce and anaconda to the chain

techniques.name: Loop Choke (from Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Use collar grips from butterfly guard to trap the opponent's neck in a loop choke when they pressure forward into the guard.

initialPosition.body: Seated in butterfly guard. Opponent is driving forward with low head position into your guard.
initialPosition.grips: Deep cross collar grip — 4 fingers inside, fed across the back of their neck to create the loop. Other hand controls their posture.
initialPosition.legs: Butterfly hooks keep opponent close and prevent them from backing out of the choke.
initialPosition.hips: Angled toward the collar grip side, falling back to tighten the loop.

greenLights: You've fed a deep collar grip across the back of their neck
Opponent is driving forward with their head low — walking into the choke
Butterfly hooks anchor them close so they can't back away
Opponent is not defending their collar
redLights: Opponent has strong upright posture — can't feed the collar deep
Collar grip is too shallow for the loop to be effective
Opponent is pulling away and hooks can't keep them close
Opponent is actively grip-fighting your collar hand
steps: From butterfly guard, feed your hand DEEP into their cross collar (4 fingers inside). Pull collar across and behind their neck to form the loop.
As opponent drives forward, tighten the loop by pulling the collar grip toward your chest. Butterfly hooks prevent them from backing out.
Fall to the collar-grip side while pulling their head into the loop. Your other hand can assist by pushing their head down.
Squeeze by pulling the collar tight across their neck. The loop compresses the carotid arteries.
complexpieces: Set up during their pass attempt — feed the collar as they try to clear your hooks
Chain with cross collar choke: if the loop doesn't finish, the grip is set for the second hand
Use the loop choke threat to set up sweeps — they posture to defend, opening sweep angles
simplifications: Practice feeding the collar grip deep behind the neck — depth is everything
Drill the loop formation against a static partner from butterfly guard
Focus on the timing: feed the collar as they drive in, not when they're pulling away

techniques.name: Crossface Pressure Pass
techniques.goal: Apply strong crossface pressure to flatten the butterfly guard player, clear their hooks, and pass to side control.

initialPosition.body: Kneeling or in combat base in opponent's butterfly guard. Driving forward with shoulder into their face/jaw.
initialPosition.grips: Crossface arm: forearm or shoulder driving across their jaw. Underhook or arm control on the other side.
initialPosition.legs: Knees tight, sprawling slightly to flatten them. Working to clear butterfly hooks with hip pressure.
initialPosition.hips: Driving LOW and FORWARD with heavy chest and shoulder pressure.

greenLights: You can establish crossface pressure — shoulder on their jaw line
Opponent's butterfly hooks are shallow and clearable
Your weight is driving them flat to their back
Opponent is defensive and not actively sweeping
redLights: Opponent has a strong underhook preventing your crossface
They're actively elevating with hooks — you're being swept
You're too upright — not enough shoulder pressure to flatten them
Opponent is transitioning to X-guard or SLX underneath you
steps: Close the distance and drive your shoulder/forearm ACROSS their jaw (crossface). Turn their head away from you.
Sprawl your hips slightly to create downward pressure. Your chest drives into their torso, flattening them.
Clear butterfly hooks by pushing one knee down with your shin or stepping over their hook.
Pass to side control with constant crossface pressure. Secure hip control once past the hooks.
complexpieces: If they re-establish a hook, switch to body lock pass while maintaining crossface
Chain with knee slice: use the crossface to set up the slice across their leg
If they underhook, switch sides and crossface the other direction
simplifications: Practice the crossface shoulder drive as a pressure drill
Drill clearing hooks while maintaining crossface — two separate skills combined
Focus on staying LOW — the most common mistake is rising up and losing pressure

techniques.name: Body Lock Pass
techniques.goal: Lock your hands around the opponent's torso from inside butterfly guard and drive forward to flatten them, clearing hooks with body lock pressure.

initialPosition.body: Kneeling in opponent's butterfly guard. Chest-to-chest, wrapping both arms around their torso.
initialPosition.grips: Both arms wrap around opponent's torso — hands clasped together behind their back in a body lock.
initialPosition.legs: Knees tight together inside their butterfly hooks. Sprawling pressure drives hooks down and out.
initialPosition.hips: Driving FORWARD and DOWN. Heavy hip pressure flattens them and pries hooks loose.

greenLights: You can lock your hands around their torso — body lock established
Opponent is unable to create space with their hooks due to your pressure
Your hip pressure is flattening their guard and clearing hooks
Opponent can't underhook or frame because your body lock traps their arms
redLights: Opponent has a strong underhook preventing the body lock
They're actively elevating with hooks — sweeping you before the lock settles
You can't close the body lock — arms aren't long enough or their frame blocks it
Opponent is transitioning to half guard or X-guard during the lock attempt
steps: Close distance and wrap both arms around opponent's torso. Clasp hands behind their back — body lock secured.
Drive hips FORWARD and DOWN with heavy pressure. Flatten them onto their back.
Clear butterfly hooks by stepping over them one at a time while maintaining the body lock. Hip switching helps clear hooks.
Pass to side control or mount once hooks are cleared. Maintain the body lock for control during the transition.
complexpieces: If they re-hook during the pass, hip switch again to clear
Chain with crossface: release one arm from body lock, establish crossface, continue passing
Use the body lock to take mount by stepping over both hooks and sitting into mount
simplifications: Practice establishing the body lock from inside butterfly guard
Drill hip switching to clear hooks against light resistance
Focus on constant forward pressure — the body lock only works with weight driving through

techniques.name: Sprawl and Smash
techniques.goal: Sprawl your hips back when the opponent tries to elevate with butterfly hooks, then drive forward to smash their guard flat and pass.

initialPosition.body: In opponent's butterfly guard. Sprawling hips BACK when they attempt to elevate. Then driving forward to smash.
initialPosition.grips: Overhook or head control to prevent their elevation. Other hand controls their arm or posts on the mat.
initialPosition.legs: Sprawled back — hips low and heavy, removing the angle for their hooks to elevate.
initialPosition.hips: Heavy and sprawled back. When hooks lose power, DRIVE forward to smash and flatten.

greenLights: Opponent is attempting the butterfly sweep — you feel the elevation coming
You can sprawl back fast enough to kill their hook power
Your hip sprawl removes the lifting angle from their hooks
Opponent is committed to the sweep and can't redirect when you sprawl
redLights: You sprawled too late and are already being elevated
Opponent switches to a different attack before you can drive forward
Your sprawl creates too much space and they transition to another guard
You don't have head or arm control to capitalize on the sprawl
steps: React to opponent's sweep attempt by SPRAWLING hips back immediately. Drop hips LOW to kill the hook's elevation power.
Establish overhook or head control to pin them as you sprawl. Their hooks lose leverage.
Once hooks are neutralized, DRIVE forward with hips and chest. Smash them flat onto their back.
Clear the weakened hooks and pass to side control or mount. Maintain constant forward pressure throughout.
complexpieces: If they switch to arm drag as you sprawl, immediately redirect to a crossface pass
Chain: sprawl (kill sweep) → body lock → hip switch → pass to side control
Use the sprawl momentum to set up a d'arce choke as their head drops
simplifications: Practice the sprawl reaction as a timing drill — feel the hooks elevate, sprawl immediately
Drill the sprawl-to-forward-drive transition separately
Focus on keeping hips LOW during the sprawl — high hips still get swept

techniques.name: Leg Drag Pass (vs Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Control one of the opponent's butterfly legs and drag it across your body to pass with tight hip control.

initialPosition.body: In opponent's butterfly guard. Grabbing one of their hooked legs to drag it across your body.
initialPosition.grips: One hand grips opponent's pant leg at the ankle/shin (hooked leg). Other hand controls collar, belt, or far leg.
initialPosition.legs: Stepping to the drag side as you pull their leg across. Strong base maintained.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and to the side, pinning their dragged leg against your hip.

greenLights: You can grab their butterfly leg and redirect it across your body
Upper body grips are manageable — the drag won't trigger a sweep
You have a secondary grip for control during the pass
Opponent is flat and not actively elevating
redLights: Opponent has a strong underhook and is actively sweeping
They're transitioning to X-guard as you reach for the leg
You can't control the leg — they keep re-inserting the hook
Opponent is sitting up aggressively and threatening sweeps
steps: From inside their butterfly guard, grab one hooked leg at the pant cuff (ankle or shin area).
DRAG the leg across your body toward your opposite hip. Step to the drag side.
PIN the dragged leg against your hip with your body weight. Drive hips close to theirs — hip-to-hip.
Drop into side control on the drag side. Their hips are turned away, making reguarding very difficult.
complexpieces: Chain with crossface: establish crossface on the drag side for immediate control
If they reguard, immediately switch to knee slice
Use the leg drag to set up a back take by stepping over to their back side
simplifications: Practice the leg grab and drag motion from inside butterfly guard
Drill the hip-to-hip pinning pressure from the drag position
Focus on the step to the drag side — footwork is critical

techniques.name: Knee Slice Pass (vs Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Clear the butterfly hooks and drive your knee across the opponent's thigh to slice through and reach side control.

initialPosition.body: In opponent's butterfly guard. Working to clear hooks and insert knee across their thigh.
initialPosition.grips: Near arm: crossface or collar grip. Far hand: controlling their pants, hook leg, or stripping grips.
initialPosition.legs: Lead knee drops across opponent's thigh after clearing the butterfly hook. Rear leg posted for driving forward.
initialPosition.hips: Driving forward and LOW through center as the knee slices across.

greenLights: You've cleared or flattened the butterfly hook enough to insert your knee across
Crossface or collar grip is established to flatten them
Their far-side hook is cleared or neutralized
Opponent is on their back and not re-inserting hooks
redLights: Butterfly hooks are still active and they're elevating
Opponent has strong underhook blocking the crossface
They're transitioning to X-guard or SLX underneath you
Opponent is sitting up aggressively while you attempt the slice
steps: Clear the butterfly hooks by sprawling, pushing them down, or stepping over. Close distance immediately.
Drop your near knee across their thigh. Secure crossface or collar grip to flatten them.
SLICE your knee forward through their legs with constant chest pressure. Keep hips LOW.
Clear past their legs and consolidate side control with crossface and hip control.
complexpieces: If they re-insert a hook, backstep or hip switch to clear again
Chain with leg drag: fake knee slice, redirect to leg drag when they react
Use the collar grip to threaten a choke during the slice to distract
simplifications: Practice clearing butterfly hooks as a separate drill
Drill the knee slice from a static position without hooks
Focus on constant shoulder pressure throughout the slice — don't rise up

techniques.name: Stack Pass (vs Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Drive the opponent's knees toward their chest from butterfly guard to collapse their structure and pass using stacking pressure.

initialPosition.body: In opponent's butterfly guard. Driving forward to collapse their leg structure and stack them.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's legs at the knees or thighs, pushing them toward their chest. Or underhooks dragging them up.
initialPosition.legs: Stepping forward. Knees driving into their legs to compress their structure.
initialPosition.hips: Driving FORWARD and DOWN, stacking opponent's hips over their shoulders.

greenLights: You can drive forward and their hooks lose elevation power
You can push their knees toward their chest effectively
Opponent is relatively flat and can't maintain distance
Upper body control prevents them from framing you away
redLights: Hooks are powerful and they're elevating you off the mat
Opponent has strong frames (arms straight) preventing you from closing distance
They're transitioning to another guard as you stack
Opponent is very flexible and stacking has minimal effect
steps: Close distance and drive forward, pushing opponent's knees toward their chest. Kill the hook elevation angle.
Stack their hips OVER their head with shoulder pressure into their thighs. Head on the passing side.
Walk around to passing side while maintaining constant stacking pressure. Don't let them unstack.
Clear the legs and drop into side control. Secure crossface and hip control.
complexpieces: Chain with leg drag: if they kick out of the stack, redirect to leg drag
If their grips prevent stacking, strip grips first then immediately drive forward
Use the stack to set up a choke (d'arce, anaconda) as their head drops during the pass
simplifications: Practice the forward stacking drive against butterfly as a pressure drill
Drill clearing hooks through stacking rather than individual hook removal
Focus on constant forward pressure — the biggest mistake is letting them re-establish distance

techniques.name: Backstep Pass (vs Butterfly)
techniques.goal: Step back and around when the opponent elevates for sweeps from butterfly guard, using the backstep to kill the hook's power and pass.

initialPosition.body: In opponent's butterfly guard. They're attempting to elevate — you step backward and around to neutralize the sweep.
initialPosition.grips: Controlling opponent's legs or upper body. Maintaining grips as you backstep to prevent them from following.
initialPosition.legs: Stepping one leg FAR back and around the hook side. Removing the hook's leverage by changing your angle.
initialPosition.hips: Turning hips away from the hook as you backstep, removing the elevation angle.

greenLights: Opponent is committing to a butterfly sweep — the elevation is coming
You can feel which hook is the sweeping hook and backstep that side
Your balance allows a controlled backstep without stumbling
Opponent is committed and can't redirect once you backstep
redLights: You're already being elevated — too late for a controlled backstep
Opponent switches to arm drag as you backstep — gives up your back
Your balance is compromised and the backstep would make you fall
Both hooks are equally active — unclear which side to backstep
steps: React to opponent's butterfly sweep attempt. Identify the sweeping-side hook.
BACKSTEP with the leg on the sweeping side — step it far back and around. Turn your hips away from the hook.
The backstep removes the hook's elevation power. Immediately drive forward to prevent reguarding.
Pass to side control or transition to a toreando/knee slice as opponent's guard is disrupted.
complexpieces: Chain with leg entanglement: backstep directly into a leg lock position instead of passing
If they follow the backstep with a re-hook, immediately hip switch and pass the other side
Use the backstep to time a crossface — as you step back, their head is exposed
simplifications: Practice the backstep reaction as a timing drill against sweep attempts
Drill the backstep footwork solo — step back, turn hips, drive forward
Focus on the immediate forward drive AFTER the backstep — don't pause at distance

positions.name: Turtle
positions.description: Defensive position where one player is on all fours. Common transitional position from failed takedowns, guard passes, and scrambles.

techniques.name: Clock Choke
techniques.goal: Choke the turtled opponent using their own collar by walking in a circular motion around their head — the rotation tightens the collar like the hands of a clock. Gi technique.

initialPosition.body: Beside and slightly behind the turtled opponent. Perpendicular to their body.
initialPosition.grips: Near hand reaches UNDER their chin and grips the far-side collar deep (4 fingers inside, knuckles against neck). Far hand grips their near-side belt or back of pants.
initialPosition.legs: Near-side knee on the mat tight against their body. Far-side foot posted on the mat for mobility and driving.
initialPosition.hips: Close to opponent's side, ready to walk in a circular motion toward their head.

greenLights: Opponent is static in turtle — not actively moving or rolling
You can feed your hand under their chin to their far-side collar
You have a strong, deep collar grip across their neck
Opponent is tight in turtle but not defending the collar — focused on protecting hooks
redLights: Opponent is actively granby rolling or sitting to guard — too dynamic to set up the choke
You can't get your hand under their chin (chin is tucked very tightly to their chest)
Opponent is hand-fighting and actively preventing collar access
Opponent is sitting out or standing up from turtle — position is changing
steps: Position yourself perpendicular to the turtle. Reach your near hand UNDER their chin and grip the far-side collar DEEP (4 fingers inside, knuckles against the artery side of the neck).
With your far hand, grip their belt or the back of their pants for anchor control. Near-side knee stays tight against their body.
Walk your legs in a CIRCULAR direction around their head — clockwise or counter-clockwise depending on your position. This rotational movement tightens the collar across their neck.
As you circle, drop your chest weight onto their upper back and continue walking. The collar cinches tighter with each step. Maintain forward pressure until they tap.
complexpieces: If they try to roll out, follow and transition to side control with the collar grip still active
Chain with back take: if they open up from turtle to defend the choke, insert your hooks for back control instead
Baseball bat choke variation: use two hands on the collar (both gripping) for a different choking angle
simplifications: Practice getting the collar grip under the chin without the choke — just the grip insertion
Walk the circular motion SLOWLY to understand how each step tightens the collar
Drill from a completely static turtle position before adding any partner movement/resistance
